**Arizona**[[c]](#cite_note-14) is a landlocked state in the [Southwestern United States](/wiki/Southwestern_United_States "Southwestern United States"), sharing the [Four Corners](/wiki/Four_Corners "Four Corners") region with [Colorado](/wiki/Colorado "Colorado"), [New Mexico](/wiki/New_Mexico "New Mexico"), and [Utah](/wiki/Utah "Utah"). It also borders [Nevada](/wiki/Nevada "Nevada") to the northwest and [California](/wiki/California "California") to the west, and shares [an international border](/wiki/Mexico-United_States_border "Mexico-United States border") with the [Mexican states](/wiki/Mexican_states "Mexican states") of [Sonora](/wiki/Sonora "Sonora") and [Baja California](/wiki/Baja_California "Baja California") to the south and southwest. Its capital and [largest city](/wiki/List_of_largest_cities "List of largest cities") is [Phoenix](/wiki/Phoenix,_Arizona "Phoenix, Arizona"), which is the most populous state capital and [fifth-most populous city](/wiki/List_of_United_States_cities_by_population "List of United States cities by population") in the United States. Arizona is divided into 15 [counties](/wiki/List_of_counties_in_Arizona "List of counties in Arizona").

Arizona is the [sixth-largest](/wiki/List_of_U.S._states_and_territories_by_area "List of U.S. states and territories by area") state by area and the [14th-most-populous](/wiki/List_of_U.S._states_and_territories_by_population "List of U.S. states and territories by population") of the 50 states. It is the 48th state and last of the [contiguous states](/wiki/Contiguous_United_States "Contiguous United States") to be admitted to the Union, achieving statehood on February 14, 1912. Historically part of the territory of [Alta California](/wiki/Alta_California "Alta California") and [Nuevo México](/wiki/Santa_Fe_de_Nuevo_M%C3%A9xico "Santa Fe de Nuevo México") in [New Spain](/wiki/New_Spain "New Spain") ([Spanish Empire](/wiki/Spanish_Empire "Spanish Empire")), it became part of independent Mexico in 1821. After being defeated in the [Mexican–American War](/wiki/Mexican%E2%80%93American_War "Mexican–American War"), Mexico ceded much of this territory to the United States in 1848, where the area became part of the [New Mexico Territory](/wiki/New_Mexico_Territory "New Mexico Territory"). The southernmost portion of the state was acquired in 1853 through the [Gadsden Purchase](/wiki/Gadsden_Purchase "Gadsden Purchase").

[Southern Arizona](/wiki/Southern_Arizona "Southern Arizona") is known for its [desert](/wiki/Desert "Desert") climate, with extremely hot summers and mild winters. [Northern Arizona](/wiki/Northern_Arizona "Northern Arizona") features forests of pine, [Douglas fir](/wiki/Douglas_fir "Douglas fir"), and [spruce](/wiki/Spruce "Spruce") trees; the [Colorado Plateau](/wiki/Colorado_Plateau "Colorado Plateau"); mountain ranges (such as the [San Francisco Mountains](/wiki/San_Francisco_Peaks "San Francisco Peaks")); as well as large, deep [canyons](/wiki/Canyon "Canyon"), with much more moderate summer temperatures and significant winter snowfalls. There are [ski resorts](/wiki/Skiing "Skiing") in the areas of [Flagstaff](/wiki/Flagstaff,_Arizona "Flagstaff, Arizona"), [Sunrise](/wiki/Sunrise_Park_Resort "Sunrise Park Resort"), and [Tucson](/wiki/Tucson,_Arizona "Tucson, Arizona"). In addition to the internationally known [Grand Canyon National Park](/wiki/Grand_Canyon_National_Park "Grand Canyon National Park"), which is one of the [world's seven natural wonders](/wiki/Seven_Natural_Wonders_of_the_World "Seven Natural Wonders of the World"), there are several [national forests](/wiki/U.S._National_Forest "U.S. National Forest"), [national parks](/wiki/National_parks_(United_States) "National parks (United States)"), and [national monuments](/wiki/National_monument_(United_States) "National monument (United States)").

Arizona is home to a diverse population. About one-quarter of the state[[12]](#cite_note-:2-15)[[13]](#cite_note-16) is made up of [Indian reservations](/wiki/List_of_Indian_reservations_in_Arizona "List of Indian reservations in Arizona") that serve as the home of [27 federally recognized Native American tribes](/wiki/Indigenous_peoples_of_Arizona#List_of_contemporary_peoples "Indigenous peoples of Arizona"), including the [Navajo Nation](/wiki/Navajo_Nation "Navajo Nation"), the largest in the state and the country, with more than 300,000 citizens. Since the 1980s, the proportion of [Hispanics](/wiki/Hispanic_and_Latino_Americans "Hispanic and Latino Americans") has grown significantly owing to migration from Mexico and Central America. A substantial portion of the population are followers of the [Roman Catholic Church](/wiki/Roman_Catholic_Church "Roman Catholic Church") and [the Church of Jesus Christ of Latter-day Saints](/wiki/The_Church_of_Jesus_Christ_of_Latter-day_Saints "The Church of Jesus Christ of Latter-day Saints"). Arizona's population and economy have grown dramatically since the 1950s because of inward migration, and the state is now a major hub of the [Sun Belt](/wiki/Sun_Belt "Sun Belt"). Many large companies, such as [PetSmart](/wiki/PetSmart "PetSmart") and [Circle K](/wiki/Circle_K "Circle K"),[[14]](#cite_note-17) have headquarters in the state, and Arizona is home to major universities, including the [University of Arizona](/wiki/University_of_Arizona "University of Arizona"), [Arizona State University](/wiki/Arizona_State_University "Arizona State University"), and [Northern Arizona University](/wiki/Northern_Arizona_University "Northern Arizona University").

## Etymology

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Arizona&action=edit&section=1 "Edit section: Etymology")]

The state's name appears to originate from an earlier Spanish name, *Arizonac*, derived from the [O'odham](/wiki/Tohono_O%27odham "Tohono O'odham") name *alĭ ṣonak*, meaning 'small spring'. Initially this term was applied by Spanish colonists only to an area near the [silver mining](/wiki/Silver_mining "Silver mining") camp of [Planchas de Plata, Sonora](/wiki/Planchas_de_Plata,_Sonora "Planchas de Plata, Sonora").[[15]](#cite_note-18)[[16]](#cite_note-19)[[17]](#cite_note-20)[[18]](#cite_note-21) To the European settlers, the O'odham pronunciation sounded like *Arissona*.[[19]](#cite_note-AZC070211-22) The area is still known as *alĭ ṣonak* in the O'odham language.[[11]](#cite_note-ood-13)

Another possible origin is the [Basque](/wiki/Basque_language "Basque language") phrase *haritz ona* 'the good oak', as there were numerous Basque shepherds in the area.[[20]](#cite_note-23)[[21]](#cite_note-24)[[22]](#cite_note-25) A native-born Mexican of Basque ancestry established the *[ranchería](/wiki/Rancher%C3%ADa "Ranchería")* (small rural settlement) of Arizona between 1734 and 1736 in the current Mexican state of [Sonora](/wiki/Sonora "Sonora"). It became notable after a significant discovery of silver there around 1737.[[23]](#cite_note-26)

## History

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Arizona&action=edit&section=2 "Edit section: History")]

Main article: [History of Arizona](/wiki/History_of_Arizona "History of Arizona")

For a chronological guide, see [Timeline of Arizona](/wiki/Timeline_of_Arizona "Timeline of Arizona").

For thousands of years before the modern era, Arizona was home to many ancient [Native American](/wiki/Indigenous_peoples_of_the_Americas "Indigenous peoples of the Americas") civilizations. [Hohokam](/wiki/Hohokam "Hohokam"), [Mogollon](/wiki/Mogollon_culture "Mogollon culture"), and [Ancestral Puebloan](/wiki/Ancestral_Puebloans "Ancestral Puebloans") cultures were among those that flourished throughout the state. Many of their pueblos, cliffside dwellings, rock paintings and other prehistoric treasures have survived and attract thousands of tourists each year.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/f/f6/La_conquista_del_Colorado.jpg/250px-La_conquista_del_Colorado.jpg)](/wiki/File:La_conquista_del_Colorado.jpg)

*La conquista del Colorado*, by [Augusto Ferrer-Dalmau](/wiki/Augusto_Ferrer-Dalmau "Augusto Ferrer-Dalmau"), depicts [Francisco Vázquez de Coronado](/wiki/Francisco_V%C3%A1zquez_de_Coronado "Francisco Vázquez de Coronado")'s 1540–1542 expedition. [García López de Cárdenas](/wiki/Garc%C3%ADa_L%C3%B3pez_de_C%C3%A1rdenas "García López de Cárdenas") can be seen overlooking the [Grand Canyon](/wiki/Grand_Canyon "Grand Canyon").

In 1539, [Marcos de Niza](/wiki/Marcos_de_Niza "Marcos de Niza"), a Spanish [Franciscan](/wiki/Franciscan "Franciscan"), became the first European to contact Native Americans. He explored parts of the present state and made contact with [native](/wiki/Native_Americans_in_the_United_States "Native Americans in the United States") inhabitants, probably the [Sobaipuri](/wiki/Sobaipuri "Sobaipuri"). The expedition of Spanish explorer [Coronado](/wiki/Francisco_V%C3%A1squez_de_Coronado "Francisco Vásquez de Coronado") entered the area in 1540–1542 during its search for [Cíbola](/wiki/Quivira_and_C%C3%ADbola "Quivira and Cíbola").[[24]](#cite_note-27) One of the first settlers in Arizona was [José Romo de Vivar](/wiki/Jos%C3%A9_Romo_de_Vivar "José Romo de Vivar").[[25]](#cite_note-Lainez_and_Torres-28)

[Father Kino](/wiki/Eusebio_Kino "Eusebio Kino") was the next European in the region under Spanish rule. A member of the [Society of Jesus](/wiki/Society_of_Jesus "Society of Jesus") ("[Jesuits](/wiki/Jesuits "Jesuits")"), he led the development of a chain of missions in the region. He converted many of the Indians to Christianity in the [Pimería Alta](/wiki/Pimer%C3%ADa_Alta "Pimería Alta") (now southern Arizona and northern [Sonora](/wiki/Sonora "Sonora")) in the 1690s and early 18th century. Spain founded *presidios* ("fortified towns") at Tubac in 1752 and Tucson in 1775.[[26]](#cite_note-29)

When Mexico achieved its independence from the [Kingdom of Spain](/wiki/Kingdom_of_Spain "Kingdom of Spain") and its [Spanish Empire](/wiki/Spanish_Empire "Spanish Empire") in 1821, what is now Arizona became part of its Territory of *Nueva California*, ("New California"), also known as *[Alta California](/wiki/Alta_California "Alta California")* ("Upper California").[[27]](#cite_note-30) Descendants of ethnic Spanish and [mestizo](/wiki/Mestizo "Mestizo") settlers from the colonial years still lived in the area at the time of the arrival of later European-American migrants from the United States.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/e/e6/Mexico_1824_%28equirectangular_projection%29.png/250px-Mexico_1824_%28equirectangular_projection%29.png)](/wiki/File:Mexico_1824_(equirectangular_projection).png)

[Mexico](/wiki/First_Mexican_Republic "First Mexican Republic") in 1824. [Alta California](/wiki/Alta_California "Alta California") is the northwesternmost state.

During the [Mexican–American War](/wiki/Mexican%E2%80%93American_War "Mexican–American War") (1847–1848), the [U.S. Army](/wiki/United_States_Army "United States Army") occupied the national capital of [Mexico City](/wiki/Mexico_City "Mexico City") and pursued its claim to much of northern Mexico, including what later became [Arizona Territory](/wiki/Territory_of_Arizona_(United_States) "Territory of Arizona (United States)") in 1863 and later the State of Arizona in 1912.[[28]](#cite_note-31) The [Treaty of Guadalupe Hidalgo](/wiki/Treaty_of_Guadalupe_Hidalgo "Treaty of Guadalupe Hidalgo") (1848) specified that, in addition to language and cultural rights of the existing inhabitants of former Mexican citizens being considered as inviolable, the sum of $15 million in compensation (equivalent to $558,173,076.92 in 2025) be paid to the Republic of Mexico.[[29]](#cite_note-32) In 1853, the U.S. acquired the land south below the [Gila River](/wiki/Gila_River "Gila River") from Mexico in the [Gadsden Purchase](/wiki/Gadsden_Purchase "Gadsden Purchase") along the southern border area as encompassing the best future southern route for a transcontinental railway.[[30]](#cite_note-33)

What is now the state of Arizona was administered by the United States government as part of the [Territory of New Mexico](/wiki/Territory_of_New_Mexico "Territory of New Mexico") from 1850 until the southern part of that region seceded from the [Union](/wiki/Union_(American_Civil_War) "Union (American Civil War)") to form the [Territory of Arizona](/wiki/Confederate_Arizona "Confederate Arizona").[[31]](#cite_note-34) This newly established territory was formally organized by the federal government of the [Confederate States](/wiki/Confederate_States_of_America "Confederate States of America") on Saturday, January 18, 1862, when President [Jefferson Davis](/wiki/Jefferson_Davis "Jefferson Davis") approved and signed *An Act to Organize the Territory of Arizona*,[[32]](#cite_note-35) marking the first official use of the name "Territory of Arizona". The Southern territory supplied the Confederate government with men, horses, and equipment. Formed in 1862, [Arizona scout companies](/wiki/Company_A,_Arizona_Rangers "Company A, Arizona Rangers") served with the [Confederate States Army](/wiki/Confederate_States_Army "Confederate States Army") during the [American Civil War](/wiki/American_Civil_War "American Civil War"). Arizona has the westernmost military engagement on record during the Civil War with the [Battle of Picacho Pass](/wiki/Battle_of_Picacho_Pass "Battle of Picacho Pass") (1862).[[33]](#cite_note-36)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/f/f1/Apache_chieff_Geronimo_%28right%29_and_his_warriors_in_1886.jpg/250px-Apache_chieff_Geronimo_%28right%29_and_his_warriors_in_1886.jpg)](/wiki/File:Apache_chieff_Geronimo_(right)_and_his_warriors_in_1886.jpg)

[Geronimo](/wiki/Geronimo "Geronimo") (far right) and his [Apache](/wiki/Apache "Apache") warriors fought against both Mexican and American settlers.

The Federal government declared a new U.S. Arizona Territory, consisting of the western half of earlier New Mexico Territory, in Washington, D.C., on February 24, 1863.[[34]](#cite_note-37) These new boundaries would later form the basis of the state. The first territorial capital, Prescott, was founded in 1864 following a gold rush to central Arizona.[[35]](#cite_note-38) The capital was later [moved to Tucson, back to Prescott, and then to its final location in Phoenix](/wiki/Arizona_Territory_capitals "Arizona Territory capitals") in a series of controversial moves as different regions of the territory gained and lost political influence with the growth and development of the territory.[[36]](#cite_note-39)

Although names including "Gadsonia", "Pimeria", "Montezuma" and "Arizuma" had been considered for the territory,[[37]](#cite_note-40) when 16th President [Abraham Lincoln](/wiki/Abraham_Lincoln "Abraham Lincoln") signed the final bill, it read "Arizona", and that name was adopted. ([Montezuma](/wiki/Montezuma_(mythology) "Montezuma (mythology)") was not derived from the [Aztec](/wiki/Aztec "Aztec") emperor, but was the sacred name of a divine hero to the [Akimel O'odham](/wiki/Akimel_O%27odham "Akimel O'odham") of the [Gila River Valley](/wiki/Gila_River_Valley "Gila River Valley"). It was probably considered – and rejected – for its sentimental value before Congress settled on the name "Arizona".)[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

[Brigham Young](/wiki/Brigham_Young "Brigham Young"), leader of [the Church of Jesus Christ of Latter-day Saints](/wiki/The_Church_of_Jesus_Christ_of_Latter-day_Saints "The Church of Jesus Christ of Latter-day Saints") in [Salt Lake City, Utah](/wiki/Salt_Lake_City "Salt Lake City"), sent [Mormons](/wiki/Mormons "Mormons") to Arizona in the mid- to late 19th century. They founded [Mesa](/wiki/Mesa,_Arizona "Mesa, Arizona"), [Snowflake](/wiki/Snowflake,_Arizona "Snowflake, Arizona"), [Heber](/wiki/Heber-Overgaard,_Arizona "Heber-Overgaard, Arizona"), [Safford](/wiki/Safford,_Arizona "Safford, Arizona"), and other towns. They also settled in the [Phoenix Valley](/wiki/Phoenix_Valley "Phoenix Valley") (or "Valley of the Sun"), [Tempe](/wiki/Tempe,_Arizona "Tempe, Arizona"), [Prescott](/wiki/Prescott,_Arizona "Prescott, Arizona"), and other areas. The Mormons settled what became [northern Arizona](/wiki/Northern_Arizona "Northern Arizona") and northern New Mexico. At the time these areas were in a part of the former [New Mexico Territory](/wiki/New_Mexico_Territory "New Mexico Territory").

During the nineteenth century, a series of gold and silver rushes occurred in the territory, the best known being the 1870s stampede to the silver bonanzas of [Tombstone, Arizona](/wiki/Tombstone,_Arizona "Tombstone, Arizona"), in southeast Arizona, also known for its legendary outlaws and lawmen.[[38]](#cite_note-41) By the late 1880s, copper production eclipsed the precious metals with the rise of copper camps like [Bisbee, Arizona](/wiki/Bisbee,_Arizona "Bisbee, Arizona"), and [Jerome, Arizona](/wiki/Jerome,_Arizona "Jerome, Arizona").[[39]](#cite_note-42)[[40]](#cite_note-43) The boom and bust economy of mining also left hundreds of [ghost towns](/wiki/Ghost_towns "Ghost towns") across the territory, but copper mining continued to prosper with the territory producing more copper than any other state by 1907, which earned Arizona the nickname "the Copper State" at the time of statehood.[[41]](#cite_note-44)[[42]](#cite_note-45) During the first years of statehood the industry experienced growing pains and labor disputes with the [Bisbee Deportation](/wiki/Bisbee_Deportation "Bisbee Deportation") of 1917 the result of a copper miners' strike.[[43]](#cite_note-46)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/c/cb/Children_of_migrant_cotton_field_workers_from_Sweetwater%2C_Oklahoma%2C_8b15324.jpg/250px-Children_of_migrant_cotton_field_workers_from_Sweetwater%2C_Oklahoma%2C_8b15324.jpg)](/wiki/File:Children_of_migrant_cotton_field_workers_from_Sweetwater,_Oklahoma,_8b15324.jpg)

Children of [Depression](/wiki/Great_Depression "Great Depression")-era migrant workers, Pinal County, 1937

### 20th century to present

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Arizona&action=edit&section=3 "Edit section: 20th century to present")]

During the [Mexican Revolution](/wiki/Mexican_Revolution "Mexican Revolution") from 1910 to 1920, several battles were fought in the Mexican towns just across the border from Arizona settlements. Throughout the revolution, many Arizonans enlisted in one of the several armies fighting in Mexico. Only two significant engagements took place on U.S. soil between U.S. and Mexican forces: [Pancho Villa](/wiki/Pancho_Villa "Pancho Villa")'s 1916 [Columbus Raid](/wiki/Battle_of_Columbus_(1916) "Battle of Columbus (1916)") in New Mexico and the [Battle of Ambos Nogales](/wiki/Battle_of_Ambos_Nogales "Battle of Ambos Nogales") in 1918 in Arizona.

After Mexican federal troops fired on U.S. soldiers, the American garrison launched an assault into [Nogales, Mexico](/wiki/Nogales,_Sonora "Nogales, Sonora"). The Mexicans eventually surrendered after both sides sustained heavy casualties. A few months earlier, just west of Nogales, an Indian War battle had occurred, considered the last engagement in the [American Indian Wars](/wiki/American_Indian_Wars "American Indian Wars"), which lasted from 1775 to 1918. U.S. soldiers stationed on the border confronted [Yaqui Indians](/wiki/Yaqui_Indians "Yaqui Indians") who were using Arizona as a base to raid the nearby Mexican settlements, as part of their wars against Mexico.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

Arizona became a U.S. state on February 14, 1912, coinciding with [Valentine's Day](/wiki/Valentine%27s_Day "Valentine's Day"). Arizona was the [48th state admitted](/wiki/List_of_U.S._states_by_date_of_admission_to_the_Union "List of U.S. states by date of admission to the Union") to the U.S. and the last of the [contiguous states](/wiki/Contiguous_United_States "Contiguous United States") to be admitted.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/e/ea/Eleanor_Roosevelt_at_Gila_River%2C_Arizona_at_Japanese-American_Internment_Center_-_NARA_-_197094.jpg/250px-Eleanor_Roosevelt_at_Gila_River%2C_Arizona_at_Japanese-American_Internment_Center_-_NARA_-_197094.jpg)](/wiki/File:Eleanor_Roosevelt_at_Gila_River,_Arizona_at_Japanese-American_Internment_Center_-_NARA_-_197094.jpg)

[Eleanor Roosevelt](/wiki/Eleanor_Roosevelt "Eleanor Roosevelt") at the Gila River relocation center, April 23, 1943

Cotton farming and copper mining, two of Arizona's most important statewide industries, suffered heavily during the [Great Depression](/wiki/Great_Depression "Great Depression").[[44]](#cite_note-47) But during the 1920s and even the 1930s, tourism began to develop as the important Arizonan industry it is today. Dude ranches, such as the K L Bar and Remuda in Wickenburg, along with the Flying V and Tanque Verde in Tucson, gave tourists the chance to take part in the flavor and activities of the "Old West". Several upscale hotels and resorts opened during this period, some of which are still top tourist draws. They include the [Arizona Biltmore Hotel](/wiki/Arizona_Biltmore_Hotel "Arizona Biltmore Hotel") in central Phoenix (opened 1929) and the Wigwam Resort on the west side of the Phoenix area (opened 1936).[[45]](#cite_note-48)[[46]](#cite_note-49)

Arizona was the site of German prisoner of war camps during World War II and [Japanese American internment camps](/wiki/Internment_of_Japanese_Americans "Internment of Japanese Americans").[[47]](#cite_note-50) Because of wartime fears of a Japanese invasion of the [U.S. West Coast](/wiki/West_Coast_of_the_United_States "West Coast of the United States") (which in fact materialized in the [Aleutian Islands Campaign](/wiki/Aleutian_Islands_Campaign "Aleutian Islands Campaign") in June 1942), from 1942 to 1945, persons of Japanese descent were forced to reside in internment camps built in the interior of the country. Many lost their homes and businesses. The camps were abolished after World War II.[[48]](#cite_note-51)

The Phoenix-area [POW camp site](/wiki/Prisoner_of_war_camp "Prisoner of war camp") for Germans was purchased after the war by the [Maytag](/wiki/Maytag "Maytag") family (of major [home appliance](/wiki/Home_appliance "Home appliance") fame). It was developed as the site of the [Phoenix Zoo](/wiki/Phoenix_Zoo "Phoenix Zoo"). A Japanese-American internment camp was on [Mount Lemmon](/wiki/Mount_Lemmon "Mount Lemmon"), just outside the state's southeastern city of Tucson. Another [POW camp](/wiki/POW_camp "POW camp") was near the [Gila River](/wiki/Gila_River "Gila River") in eastern [Yuma County](/wiki/Yuma_County,_Arizona "Yuma County, Arizona"). Arizona was also home to the [Phoenix Indian School](/wiki/Steele_Indian_School_Park "Steele Indian School Park"), one of several federal [Indian boarding schools](/wiki/Indian_boarding_schools "Indian boarding schools") designed to assimilate Native American children into mainstream European-American culture. Children were often enrolled in these schools against the wishes of their parents and families. Attempts to suppress native identities included forcing the children to cut their hair, to take and use English names, to speak only English, and to practice Christianity rather than their native religions.[[49]](#cite_note-52)

Numerous Native Americans from Arizona fought for the United States during World War II. Their experiences resulted in a rising activism in the postwar years to achieve better treatment and civil rights after their return to the state. After Maricopa County did not allow them to register to vote, in 1948 veteran Frank Harrison and Harry Austin, of the [Mojave-Apache Tribe](/wiki/Mohave_people "Mohave people") at [Fort McDowell Indian Reservation](/wiki/Fort_McDowell_Indian_Reservation "Fort McDowell Indian Reservation"), brought a legal suit, *[Harrison and Austin v. Laveen](/wiki/Harrison_and_Austin_v._Laveen "Harrison and Austin v. Laveen")*, to challenge this exclusion. The Arizona Supreme Court ruled in their favor.[[50]](#cite_note-HAvote-53)

Arizona's population grew tremendously with residential and business development after World War II, aided by the widespread use of [air conditioning](/wiki/Air_conditioning "Air conditioning"), which made the intensely hot summers more comfortable. According to the *Arizona Blue Book* (published by the [Arizona Secretary of State](/wiki/Arizona_Secretary_of_State "Arizona Secretary of State")'s office each year), the state population in 1910 was 294,353. By 1970, it was 1,752,122. The percentage growth each decade averaged about 20% in the earlier decades, and about 60% each decade thereafter.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

In the 1960s, [retirement communities](/wiki/Retirement_communities "Retirement communities") were developed. These age-restricted subdivisions catered exclusively to the needs of senior citizens and attracted many retirees who wanted to escape the harsh winters of the [Midwest](/wiki/Midwest "Midwest") and the [Northeast](/wiki/Northeastern_United_States "Northeastern United States"). [Sun City](/wiki/Sun_City,_Arizona "Sun City, Arizona"), established by developer [Del Webb](/wiki/Del_Webb "Del Webb") and opened in 1960, was one of the first such communities. [Green Valley](/wiki/Green_Valley,_Arizona "Green Valley, Arizona"), south of Tucson, was another such community, designed as a retirement subdivision for Arizona's teachers. Many senior citizens from across the United States and Canada come to Arizona each winter and stay only during the winter months; they are referred to as [snowbirds](/wiki/Snowbird_(people) "Snowbird (people)").[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

In March 2000, Arizona was the site of the first legally binding election ever held over the internet to nominate a candidate for public office.[[51]](#cite_note-54) In the 2000 Arizona Democratic Primary, under worldwide attention, [Al Gore](/wiki/Al_Gore "Al Gore") defeated [Bill Bradley](/wiki/Bill_Bradley "Bill Bradley"). Voter turnout in this state primary increased more than 500% over the 1996 primary.

In the 21st century, Arizona has frequently garnered national attention for its efforts to quell illegal immigration into the state. In 2004, voters passed [Proposition 200](/wiki/2004_Arizona_Proposition_200 "2004 Arizona Proposition 200"), requiring proof of citizenship to register to vote. The [Supreme Court of the United States](/wiki/Supreme_Court_of_the_United_States "Supreme Court of the United States") struck this restriction down in 2013.[[52]](#cite_note-55) In 2010, Arizona enacted [SB 1070](/wiki/Arizona_SB_1070 "Arizona SB 1070") which required all immigrants to carry immigration papers at all times, but the Supreme Court also invalidated parts of this law in *[Arizona v. United States](/wiki/Arizona_v._United_States "Arizona v. United States")* in 2012.[[53]](#cite_note-56)

On January 8, 2011, a [gunman shot](/wiki/2011_Tucson_shooting "2011 Tucson shooting") congresswoman [Gabby Giffords](/wiki/Gabby_Giffords "Gabby Giffords") and 18 others at a gathering in Tucson. Giffords was critically wounded. The incident sparked national attention regarding incendiary political rhetoric.[[54]](#cite_note-57)

Three ships named [USS *Arizona*](/wiki/USS_Arizona "USS Arizona") have been christened in honor of the state, although only [USS *Arizona* (BB-39)](/wiki/USS_Arizona_(BB-39) "USS Arizona (BB-39)") was so named after statehood was achieved.

## Geography

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Arizona&action=edit&section=4 "Edit section: Geography")]

Main article: [Geography of Arizona](/wiki/Geography_of_Arizona "Geography of Arizona")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/e/ef/Arizona_K%C3%B6ppen.svg/250px-Arizona_K%C3%B6ppen.svg.png)](/wiki/File:Arizona_K%C3%B6ppen.svg)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/f/f9/USA_09847_Grand_Canyon_Luca_Galuzzi_2007.jpg/250px-USA_09847_Grand_Canyon_Luca_Galuzzi_2007.jpg)](/wiki/File:USA_09847_Grand_Canyon_Luca_Galuzzi_2007.jpg)

The [Grand Canyon](/wiki/Grand_Canyon "Grand Canyon")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/7/7d/Arizona_municipalities.svg/250px-Arizona_municipalities.svg.png)](/wiki/File:Arizona_municipalities.svg)

Arizona municipalities

Arizona is in the Southwestern United States as one of the [Four Corners](/wiki/Four_Corners "Four Corners") states. Arizona is the sixth [largest state by area](/wiki/List_of_U.S._states_and_territories_by_area "List of U.S. states and territories by area"), ranked after [New Mexico](/wiki/New_Mexico "New Mexico") and before [Nevada](/wiki/Nevada "Nevada"). Of the state's 113,998 square miles (295,000 km2), approximately 15% is privately owned. The remaining area is public forest and parkland, [state trust land](/wiki/Arizona_State_Trust_Land "Arizona State Trust Land") and Native American reservations. There are 24 [National Park Service](/wiki/National_Park_Service "National Park Service") maintained sites in Arizona, including the three national parks of [Grand Canyon National Park](/wiki/Grand_Canyon_National_Park "Grand Canyon National Park"), [Saguaro National Park](/wiki/Saguaro_National_Park "Saguaro National Park"), and the [Petrified Forest National Park](/wiki/Petrified_Forest_National_Park "Petrified Forest National Park").[[55]](#cite_note-58)

Arizona is well known for its [desert](/wiki/Desert "Desert") [Basin and Range](/wiki/Basin_and_Range_Province "Basin and Range Province") region in the state's southern portions, which is rich in a [landscape](/wiki/Landscape "Landscape") of [xerophyte](/wiki/Xerophyte "Xerophyte") plants such as the [cactus](/wiki/Cactus "Cactus"). This region's topography was shaped by prehistoric [volcanism](/wiki/Volcanism "Volcanism"), followed by the cooling-off and related [subsidence](/wiki/Subsidence "Subsidence"). Its climate has exceptionally hot summers and mild winters. The state is less well known for its pine-covered north-central portion of the high country of the [Colorado Plateau](/wiki/Colorado_Plateau "Colorado Plateau") (see [Arizona Mountains forests](/wiki/Arizona_Mountains_forests "Arizona Mountains forests")).

Like other states of the [Southwest](/wiki/Southwestern_United_States "Southwestern United States") United States, Arizona is marked by high mountains, the Colorado plateau, and mesas. Despite the state's aridity, 27% of Arizona is forest,[[56]](#cite_note-59) a percentage comparable to modern-day Romania or Greece.[[57]](#cite_note-60) The world's largest stand of [ponderosa pine](/wiki/Ponderosa_pine "Ponderosa pine") trees is in Arizona.[[58]](#cite_note-61)

The [Mogollon Rim](/wiki/Mogollon_Rim "Mogollon Rim") ([/moʊɡəˈjoʊn/](/wiki/Help:IPA/English "Help:IPA/English")), a 1,998-foot (609 m) [escarpment](/wiki/Escarpment "Escarpment"), cuts across the state's central section and marks the southwestern edge of the Colorado Plateau. In 2002, this was an area of the [Rodeo–Chediski Fire](/wiki/Rodeo%E2%80%93Chediski_Fire "Rodeo–Chediski Fire"), the worst fire in state history until 2011.

Located in northern Arizona, the [Grand Canyon](/wiki/Grand_Canyon "Grand Canyon") is a colorful, deep, steep-sided gorge, carved by the [Colorado River](/wiki/Colorado_River_(U.S.) "Colorado River (U.S.)"). The canyon is one of the [Seven Natural Wonders of the World](/wiki/Seven_Natural_Wonders_of_the_World "Seven Natural Wonders of the World") and is largely contained in the [Grand Canyon National Park](/wiki/Grand_Canyon_National_Park "Grand Canyon National Park") – one of the first national parks in the United States. President [Theodore Roosevelt](/wiki/Theodore_Roosevelt "Theodore Roosevelt") was a major proponent of designating the Grand Canyon area as a National Park, often visiting to hunt [mountain lion](/wiki/Cougar "Cougar") and enjoy the scenery. The canyon was created by the Colorado River cutting a channel over millions of years, and is about 277 miles (446 km) long, ranges in width from 4 to 18 miles (6 to 29 km) and attains a depth of more than 1 mile (1.6 km). Nearly two billion years of the Earth's history have been exposed as the Colorado River and its tributaries cut through layer after layer of sediment as the Colorado Plateau uplifted.

Arizona is home to one of the most well-preserved [meteorite](/wiki/Meteorite "Meteorite") impact sites in the world. Created around 50,000 years ago, the Barringer Meteorite Crater (better known simply as "[Meteor Crater](/wiki/Meteor_Crater "Meteor Crater")") is a gigantic hole in the middle of the high plains of the Colorado Plateau, about 25 miles (40 km) west of [Winslow](/wiki/Winslow,_Arizona "Winslow, Arizona").[[59]](#cite_note-62) A rim of smashed and jumbled boulders, some of them the size of small houses, rises 150 feet (46 m) above the level of the surrounding plain. The crater itself is nearly a mile (1.6 kilometers) wide and 570 feet (170 m) deep.

Arizona is one of two U.S. states, along with Hawaii, that does not observe [Daylight Saving Time](/wiki/Daylight_Saving_Time "Daylight Saving Time"), though the large [Navajo Nation](/wiki/Navajo_Nation "Navajo Nation") in the state's northeastern region does.

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/f/f2/Scottsdale_cityscape4.jpg/250px-Scottsdale_cityscape4.jpg)](/wiki/File:Scottsdale_cityscape4.jpg)

View of suburban development in [Scottsdale](/wiki/Scottsdale,_Arizona "Scottsdale, Arizona"), 2006

[Phoenix](/wiki/Phoenix,_Arizona "Phoenix, Arizona"), in [Maricopa County](/wiki/Maricopa_County,_Arizona "Maricopa County, Arizona"), is Arizona's capital and largest city. Other prominent cities in the Phoenix metro area include [Mesa](/wiki/Mesa,_Arizona "Mesa, Arizona") (Arizona's third largest city), [Chandler](/wiki/Chandler,_Arizona "Chandler, Arizona") (Arizona's fourth largest city), [Glendale](/wiki/Glendale,_Arizona "Glendale, Arizona"), [Peoria](/wiki/Peoria,_Arizona "Peoria, Arizona"), [Buckeye](/wiki/Buckeye,_Arizona "Buckeye, Arizona"), [Sun City](/wiki/Sun_City,_Arizona "Sun City, Arizona"), [Sun City West](/wiki/Sun_City_West,_Arizona "Sun City West, Arizona"), [Fountain Hills](/wiki/Fountain_Hills,_Arizona "Fountain Hills, Arizona"), [Surprise](/wiki/Surprise,_Arizona "Surprise, Arizona"), [Gilbert](/wiki/Gilbert,_Arizona "Gilbert, Arizona"), [El Mirage](/wiki/El_Mirage,_Arizona "El Mirage, Arizona"), [Avondale](/wiki/Avondale,_Arizona "Avondale, Arizona"), [Tempe](/wiki/Tempe,_Arizona "Tempe, Arizona"), [Tolleson](/wiki/Tolleson,_Arizona "Tolleson, Arizona") and [Scottsdale](/wiki/Scottsdale,_Arizona "Scottsdale, Arizona"), with a total metropolitan population of just over 4.7 million.[[60]](#cite_note-63) The average high temperature in July, 106 °F (41 °C), is one of the highest of any metropolitan area in the United States, offset by an average January high temperature of 67 °F (19 °C), the basis of its winter appeal.

[Tucson](/wiki/Tucson,_Arizona "Tucson, Arizona"), with a metro population of just over one million, is the state's second-largest city. Located in [Pima County](/wiki/Pima_County,_Arizona "Pima County, Arizona"), approximately 110 miles (180 km) southeast of Phoenix, it was incorporated in 1877, making it the oldest incorporated city in Arizona. It is home to the [University of Arizona](/wiki/University_of_Arizona "University of Arizona"). Major incorporated suburbs of Tucson include [Oro Valley](/wiki/Oro_Valley,_Arizona "Oro Valley, Arizona") and [Marana](/wiki/Marana,_Arizona "Marana, Arizona") northwest of the city, [Sahuarita](/wiki/Sahuarita,_Arizona "Sahuarita, Arizona") south of the city, and [South Tucson](/wiki/South_Tucson,_Arizona "South Tucson, Arizona") in an enclave south of downtown. It has an average July temperature of 100 °F (38 °C) and winter temperatures averaging 65 °F (18 °C). [Saguaro National Park](/wiki/Saguaro_National_Park "Saguaro National Park"), just west of the city in the [Tucson Mountains](/wiki/Tucson_Mountains "Tucson Mountains"), is the site of the world's largest collection of [Saguaro](/wiki/Saguaro "Saguaro") cacti.

The [Prescott](/wiki/Prescott,_Arizona "Prescott, Arizona") metropolitan area includes the cities of Prescott, [Cottonwood](/wiki/Cottonwood,_Arizona "Cottonwood, Arizona"), [Camp Verde](/wiki/Camp_Verde,_Arizona "Camp Verde, Arizona") and many other towns in the 8,123 square miles (21,000 km2) of [Yavapai County](/wiki/Yavapai_County "Yavapai County") area. With 212,635 residents, this cluster of towns is the state's third largest metropolitan area. The city of Prescott (population 41,528) lies approximately 100 miles (160 km) northwest of the Phoenix metropolitan area. Situated in pine tree forests at an elevation of about 5,500 feet (1,700 m), Prescott enjoys a much cooler climate than Phoenix, with average summer highs around 88 °F (31 °C) and winter temperatures averaging 50 °F (10 °C).

[Yuma](/wiki/Yuma,_Arizona "Yuma, Arizona") is the center of the fourth-largest metropolitan area in Arizona. Located in [Yuma County](/wiki/Yuma_County,_Arizona "Yuma County, Arizona"), it is near the borders of California and Mexico. It is one of the hottest cities in the United States, with an average July high of 107 °F (42 °C). For comparison, the same month's average in [Death Valley](/wiki/Death_Valley "Death Valley") is 115 °F (46 °C). The city features sunny days about 90% of the year. The Yuma [Metropolitan Statistical Area](/wiki/Metropolitan_Statistical_Area "Metropolitan Statistical Area") has a population of 160,000. Yuma attracts many winter visitors from all over the United States.

[Flagstaff](/wiki/Flagstaff,_Arizona "Flagstaff, Arizona"), in [Coconino County](/wiki/Coconino_County,_Arizona "Coconino County, Arizona"), is the largest city in northern Arizona, and is at an elevation of nearly 7,000 feet (2,100 m). With its large Ponderosa pine forests, snowy winter weather and picturesque mountains, it is a stark contrast to the desert regions typically associated with Arizona. It is sited at the base of the [San Francisco Peaks](/wiki/San_Francisco_Peaks "San Francisco Peaks"), the highest mountain range in the state of Arizona, which contains [Humphreys Peak](/wiki/Humphreys_Peak "Humphreys Peak"), the highest point in Arizona at 12,633 feet (3,851 m). Flagstaff has a strong tourism sector, due to its proximity to numerous tourist attractions including: [Grand Canyon National Park](/wiki/Grand_Canyon_National_Park "Grand Canyon National Park"), [Sedona](/wiki/Sedona,_Arizona "Sedona, Arizona"), and [Oak Creek Canyon](/wiki/Oak_Creek_Canyon "Oak Creek Canyon"). Historic [U.S. Route 66](/wiki/U.S._Route_66 "U.S. Route 66") is the main east–west street in the town. The Flagstaff metropolitan area is home to 134,421 residents and the main campus of [Northern Arizona University](/wiki/Northern_Arizona_University "Northern Arizona University").

[Lake Havasu City](/wiki/Lake_Havasu_City,_Arizona "Lake Havasu City, Arizona"), in [Mohave County](/wiki/Mohave_County,_Arizona "Mohave County, Arizona"), known as "Arizona's playground", was developed on the Colorado River and is named after Lake Havasu. Lake Havasu City has a population of about 57,000 people. It is famous for huge spring break parties, sunsets and the [London Bridge](/wiki/London_Bridge "London Bridge"), relocated from London, England. Lake Havasu City was founded by real estate developer [Robert P. McCulloch](/wiki/Robert_P._McCulloch "Robert P. McCulloch") in 1963.[[61]](#cite_note-64) It has two colleges, [Mohave Community College](/wiki/Mohave_Community_College "Mohave Community College") and ASU Colleges in Lake Havasu City.[[62]](#cite_note-65)

## Climate

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Arizona&action=edit&section=7 "Edit section: Climate")]

Further information: [Climate change in Arizona](/wiki/Climate_change_in_Arizona "Climate change in Arizona")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/2/21/Koppen-Geiger_Map_v2_USA_Arizona_1991%E2%80%932020.svg/250px-Koppen-Geiger_Map_v2_USA_Arizona_1991%E2%80%932020.svg.png)](/wiki/File:Koppen-Geiger_Map_v2_USA_Arizona_1991%E2%80%932020.svg)

[Köppen](/wiki/K%C3%B6ppen_climate_classification "Köppen climate classification") climate map of Arizona.

Due to its large area and variations in elevation, the state has a wide variety of localized climate conditions. In the lower elevations the climate is primarily desert, with mild winters and extremely hot summers. Typically, from late fall to early spring, the weather is mild, averaging a minimum of 60 °F (16 °C). November through February are the coldest months, with temperatures typically ranging from 40 to 75 °F (4 to 24 °C), with occasional frosts.[[64]](#cite_note-wrcc-67)

About midway through February, the temperatures start to rise, with warm days, and cool, breezy nights. The summer months of June through September bring a dry heat from 90 to 120 °F (32 to 49 °C), with occasional high temperatures exceeding 125 °F (52 °C) having been observed in the desert area.[[64]](#cite_note-wrcc-67) Arizona's all-time record high is 128 °F (53 °C) recorded at [Lake Havasu City](/wiki/Lake_Havasu_City,_Arizona "Lake Havasu City, Arizona") on June 29, 1994, and July 5, 2007; the all-time record low of −40 °F (−40 °C) was recorded at [Hawley Lake](/wiki/Hawley_Lake,_Arizona "Hawley Lake, Arizona") on January 7, 1971.[[65]](#cite_note-68)

Due to the primarily dry climate, large diurnal temperature variations occur in less-developed areas of the desert above 2,500 ft (760 m). The swings can be as large as 83 °F (46 °C) in the summer months. In the state's urban centers, the effects of [local warming](/wiki/Urban_heat_island "Urban heat island") result in much higher measured night-time lows than in the recent past.

Arizona has an average annual rainfall of 12.7 in (323 mm),[[66]](#cite_note-69) which comes during two rainy seasons, with [cold fronts](/wiki/Cold_front "Cold front") coming from the Pacific Ocean during the winter and a [monsoon](/wiki/North_American_Monsoon "North American Monsoon") in the summer.[[67]](#cite_note-USGShyd-70) The monsoon season occurs toward the end of summer. In July or August, the [dewpoint](/wiki/Dewpoint "Dewpoint") rises dramatically for a brief period. During this time, the air contains large amounts of [water vapor](/wiki/Water_vapor "Water vapor"). Dewpoints as high as 81 °F (27 °C)[[68]](#cite_note-Wunderground_archive_of_PHX_airport_data-71) have been recorded during the [Phoenix](/wiki/Phoenix,_Arizona "Phoenix, Arizona") monsoon season. This hot moisture brings [lightning](/wiki/Lightning "Lightning"), [thunderstorms](/wiki/Thunderstorm "Thunderstorm"), wind, and torrential, if usually brief, downpours. These downpours often cause [flash floods](/wiki/Flash_flood "Flash flood"), which can turn deadly. In an attempt to deter drivers from crossing flooding streams, the [Arizona Legislature](/wiki/Arizona_Legislature "Arizona Legislature") enacted the [Stupid Motorist Law](/wiki/Stupid_Motorist_Law "Stupid Motorist Law"). It is rare for [tornadoes](/wiki/Tornado "Tornado") or [hurricanes](/wiki/List_of_Arizona_hurricanes "List of Arizona hurricanes") to occur in Arizona.

Arizona's northern third is a [plateau](/wiki/Plateau "Plateau") at significantly higher altitudes than the lower desert, and has an appreciably cooler climate, with cold winters and mild summers, though the climate remains semiarid to arid. Extremely low temperatures are not unknown; cold air systems from the northern states and Canada occasionally push into the state, bringing temperatures below 0 °F (−18 °C) to the state's northern parts.[[69]](#cite_note-72)

Indicative of the variation in climate, Arizona is the state which has both the metropolitan area with the most days over 100 °F (38 °C) ([Phoenix](/wiki/Phoenix,_Arizona "Phoenix, Arizona")), and the metropolitan area in the lower 48 states with the most days with a low temperature below freezing ([Flagstaff](/wiki/Flagstaff,_Arizona "Flagstaff, Arizona")).[[70]](#cite_note-73)

Arizona is considered as relatively vulnerable to climate change, which can make six of Arizona's 15 counties uninhabitable for humans by the years 2040–2060. This was the cautionary warning made by a December 2020 study released by ProPublica and Rhodium Group.[[72]](#cite_note-75)

The [United States Census Bureau](/wiki/United_States_Census_Bureau "United States Census Bureau") records Arizona's population as 7,151,502 in the 2020 census,[[73]](#cite_note-Census2010-76) a 12% increase since the [2010 United States census](/wiki/2010_United_States_census "2010 United States census").[[73]](#cite_note-Census2010-76)

Arizona remained sparsely settled for most of the 19th century.[[75]](#cite_note-78) The 1860 census reported the population of "Arizona County" to be 6,482, of whom 4,040 were listed as "Indians", 21 as "free colored", and 2,421 as "white".[[76]](#cite_note-79)[[77]](#cite_note-80) Arizona's continued [population growth](/wiki/Population_growth "Population growth") has put an enormous stress on the state's water supply.[[78]](#cite_note-81) As of 2011[[update]](https://en.wikipedia.org/w/index.php?title=Arizona&action=edit), 61% of Arizona's children under age one belonged to racial groups of color.[[79]](#cite_note-82)

The population of metropolitan Phoenix increased by 45% from 1991 through 2001, helping to make Arizona the second fastest-growing state in the U.S. in the 1990s (the fastest was [Nevada](/wiki/Nevada "Nevada")).[[80]](#cite_note-83)

According to the 2010 United States census, Arizona had a population of 6,392,017. In 2010, [illegal immigrants](/wiki/Illegal_immigration "Illegal immigration") constituted an estimated 8% of the population. This was the second highest percentage of any state in the U.S.[[81]](#cite_note-84)[[d]](#cite_note-85)

Metropolitan Phoenix (4.7 million) and Tucson (1.0 million) are home to about five-sixths of Arizona's people (as of the 2010 census). Metro Phoenix alone accounts for two-thirds of the state's population.

According to [HUD](/wiki/United_States_Department_of_Housing_and_Urban_Development "United States Department of Housing and Urban Development")'s 2022 [Annual Homeless Assessment Report](/wiki/Annual_Homeless_Assessment_Report_to_Congress "Annual Homeless Assessment Report to Congress"), there were an estimated 13,553 [homeless](/wiki/Homelessness "Homelessness") people living in Arizona.[[82]](#cite_note-86)[[83]](#cite_note-87)

As of 2018, the top countries of origin for Arizona's immigrant population were Mexico, Canada, India, the [Philippines](/wiki/Philippines "Philippines") and China.[[84]](#cite_note-88)

A Navajo man on horseback in [Monument Valley](/wiki/Monument_Valley "Monument Valley")

As of 2010[[update]](https://en.wikipedia.org/w/index.php?title=Arizona&action=edit), 73% (4,215,749) of Arizona residents age five and older spoke only English at home, while 21% (1,202,638) spoke Spanish, 2% (85,602) [Navajo](/wiki/Navajo_language "Navajo language"), <1% (22,592) German, <1% (22,426) Chinese (which includes [Mandarin](/wiki/Standard_Chinese "Standard Chinese")), <1% (19,015) [Tagalog](/wiki/Tagalog_language "Tagalog language"), <1% (17,603) Vietnamese, <1% (15,707) [Other North American Indigenous Languages](/wiki/Indigenous_languages_of_North_America "Indigenous languages of North America") (especially [indigenous languages of Arizona](/wiki/Indigenous_languages_of_Arizona "Indigenous languages of Arizona")), and French was spoken as a [main language](/wiki/Main_language "Main language") by <1% (15,062) of the population over the age of five. In total, 27% (1,567,548) of Arizona's population age five and older spoke a [mother language](/wiki/Mother_language "Mother language") other than English.[[94]](#cite_note-MLA_Data-99)

Arizona is home to the largest number of speakers of [Native American languages](/wiki/Indigenous_languages_of_the_Americas "Indigenous languages of the Americas") in the 48 contiguous states, as more than 85,000 individuals reported speaking [Navajo](/wiki/Navajo_language "Navajo language"),[[94]](#cite_note-MLA_Data-99) and 10,403 people reported [Apache](/wiki/Western_Apache_language "Western Apache language"), as a language spoken at home in 2005.[[94]](#cite_note-MLA_Data-99) Arizona's [Apache County](/wiki/Apache_County,_Arizona "Apache County, Arizona") has the highest concentration of speakers of Native American Indian languages in the United States.[[95]](#cite_note-100)

The 2010 U.S. Religion Census: Religious Congregations & Membership Study by [ARDA](/wiki/Association_of_Religion_Data_Archives "Association of Religion Data Archives") reported that the three largest denominational groups in Arizona were the Catholic Church, the Church of Jesus Christ of Latter-Day Saints and non-denominational Evangelical Protestants. The Catholic Church had the highest number of adherents in Arizona (at 930,001), followed by [the Church of Jesus Christ of Latter-day Saints](/wiki/The_Church_of_Jesus_Christ_of_Latter-day_Saints "The Church of Jesus Christ of Latter-day Saints") with 392,918 members reported and then non-denominational Evangelical Protestant churches, reporting 281,105 adherents. The religious body with the largest number of congregations is The Church of Jesus Christ of Latter-day Saints (with 811 congregations) followed by the [Southern Baptist Convention](/wiki/Southern_Baptist_Convention "Southern Baptist Convention") (with 323 congregations).[[97]](#cite_note-102) This census accounted for about 2.4 million of Arizona's 6.4 million residents in 2010.

Hinduism became the largest non-Christian religion (when combining all denominations) in 2010 with more than 32,000 adherents, followed by Judaism with more than 20,000 and Buddhism with more than 19,000.[[98]](#cite_note-ARDA1-103)[[102]](#cite_note-108)[[103]](#cite_note-109)

By the publication of the [Public Religion Research Institute](/wiki/Public_Religion_Research_Institute "Public Religion Research Institute")'s 2020 study, 68% of the population identified as Christian.[[104]](#cite_note-110) At the Pew Research Center's 2014 study, 67% of Arizona was Christian.[[105]](#cite_note-111) The irreligious or agnostic (or unaffiliated with any particular type of religion) population of 2014 to 2022 across three surveys ranged from 24% to 31% of Arizonans, in each survey forming a larger proportion than in most other U.S. states.

A third study by the Association of Religion Data Archives in 2020 also determined Christianity as the most prevalent religion in the state, with Catholics numbering 1,522,410 adherents and non-denominational Christians increasing to 402,842 Arizonan Christians.[[106]](#cite_note-112)

### Native American tribes

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Arizona&action=edit&section=12 "Edit section: Native American tribes")]

At the onset of European colonization what is now Arizona was inhabited by many Native American tribes, they included mainly the Navajo, the Apache, the Akimel O'odham, the Cocopah, the Halchidhoma, the Havasupai, Yavapai, and Hualapai, the Hopi, the Jocome and Jano, the Maricopa, the Mohave, the southern Paiute, the Tohono O'odham, the Yaqui, the Yuma and the Zuni Pueblo.[[107]](#cite_note-113) Some of them still live in the state today.

## Economy

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Arizona&action=edit&section=13 "Edit section: Economy")]

See also: [Economy of Arizona](/wiki/Economy_of_Arizona "Economy of Arizona") and [Arizona locations by per capita income](/wiki/Arizona_locations_by_per_capita_income "Arizona locations by per capita income")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/0/0c/Barringer_Crater_aerial_photo_by_USGS.jpg/250px-Barringer_Crater_aerial_photo_by_USGS.jpg)](/wiki/File:Barringer_Crater_aerial_photo_by_USGS.jpg)

Arizona's [Meteor Crater](/wiki/Meteor_Crater "Meteor Crater") is a tourist attraction.

Arizona's total [gross state product](/wiki/Gross_state_product "Gross state product") in 2023 was $508 billion.[[109]](#cite_note-GDPByState-115) The composition of the state's economy is moderately diverse, although health care, transportation and the government remain the largest sectors.[[110]](#cite_note-116)

Arizona's per capita income was $61,652 in 2023, ranking 33rd in the U.S.[[111]](#cite_note-r095-117) The state's 2023 [median household income](/wiki/Median_household_income "Median household income") was $74,568, ranking 19th in the country and just below the U.S. national mean.[[112]](#cite_note-u858-118)

Early in its history, Arizona's economy relied on the "five C's": copper (see *[Copper mining in Arizona](/wiki/Copper_mining_in_Arizona "Copper mining in Arizona")*), cotton, cattle, [citrus](/wiki/Citrus "Citrus"), and [climate](/wiki/Climate "Climate") (tourism). Copper is still extensively mined from many expansive open-pit and underground mines, accounting for two-thirds of the nation's output.

### Employment

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Arizona&action=edit&section=14 "Edit section: Employment")]

* Total employment (April 2025): 3,633,200[[113]](#cite_note-:0-119)
* Total employer establishments (2016): 139,134[[114]](#cite_note-120)[*[needs update](/wiki/Wikipedia:Manual_of_Style/Dates_and_numbers#Chronological_items "Wikipedia:Manual of Style/Dates and numbers")*]

In 2025, 99.5% of Arizona businesses were [small businesses](/wiki/Small_business "Small business") and employed 42.% of the state's work force.[[115]](#cite_note-121)

### Agriculture

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Arizona&action=edit&section=16 "Edit section: Agriculture")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/4/4b/NRCSAZ02010_-_Arizona_%28303%29%28NRCS_Photo_Gallery%29.tif/lossless-page1-250px-NRCSAZ02010_-_Arizona_%28303%29%28NRCS_Photo_Gallery%29.tif.png)](/wiki/File:NRCSAZ02010_-_Arizona_(303)(NRCS_Photo_Gallery).tif)

[Romaine](/wiki/Romaine_lettuce "Romaine lettuce") farms in [Yuma](/wiki/Yuma,_Arizona "Yuma, Arizona")

Multiple crops are grown in Arizona, including [durum wheat](/wiki/Durum_wheat "Durum wheat"), [lettuce](/wiki/Leaf_lettuce "Leaf lettuce"), [spinach](/wiki/Spinach "Spinach"), [cantaloupe](/wiki/Cantaloupe "Cantaloupe"), [broccoli](/wiki/Broccoli "Broccoli"), [cauliflower](/wiki/Cauliflower "Cauliflower"), [cabbage](/wiki/Cabbage "Cabbage"), and [watermelon](/wiki/Watermelon "Watermelon").[[118]](#cite_note-quick-stats-124)

Federal [crop insurance](/wiki/Crop_insurance "Crop insurance") is available for grape (*[Vitis vinifera](/wiki/Vitis_vinifera "Vitis vinifera")* and other *[Vitis](/wiki/Vitis "Vitis")* spp.) here.[[119]](#cite_note-Federal-insurance-gr-125) Together with [California's crop](/wiki/California_grape "California grape") it falls under special provisions of the relevant crop insurance statutes.[[119]](#cite_note-Federal-insurance-gr-125) [Insect pests](/w/index.php?title=Insect_pest_of_grape&action=edit&redlink=1 "Insect pest of grape (page does not exist)") and [diseases](/wiki/Disease_of_grape "Disease of grape") are [covered](/wiki/Crop_insurance_coverage "Crop insurance coverage"), excluding Phylloxera (*[Daktulosphaira vitifoliae](/wiki/Daktulosphaira_vitifoliae "Daktulosphaira vitifoliae")*) or failure to correctly [apply insect control](/wiki/Control_of_insects "Control of insects") or [apply disease control](/wiki/Application_of_pesticide "Application of pesticide").[[119]](#cite_note-Federal-insurance-gr-125)

The whitefly [*Bemisia tabaci* B](/wiki/Bemisia_tabaci_B "Bemisia tabaci B") was introduced through the [poinsettia](/wiki/Poinsettia "Poinsettia") trade in the 1980s, displacing the previous [A biotype](/wiki/Bemisia_tabaci_A "Bemisia tabaci A").[[120]](#cite_note-White-Fly-126) In 2004 the [Q biotype](/wiki/Bemisia_tabaci_Q "Bemisia tabaci Q") (from the Mediterranean) was first found here, also on poinsettia.[[120]](#cite_note-White-Fly-126)

The Colorado potato beetle (*[Leptinotarsa decemlineata](/wiki/Leptinotarsa_decemlineata "Leptinotarsa decemlineata")*) is either native or an early [introduction](/wiki/Introduced_species "Introduced species") here.[[121]](#cite_note-L-dec-127) Unusually, the population here commonly feeds on Silverleaf nightshade (*[Solanum elaeagnifolium](/wiki/Solanum_elaeagnifolium "Solanum elaeagnifolium")*), which is usually a less attractive host for this beetle.[[121]](#cite_note-L-dec-127) The CPB is an occasional pest of tomato.[[121]](#cite_note-L-dec-127)

In addition, the various urban areas are served by complex networks of [state routes](/wiki/List_of_Arizona_State_Routes "List of Arizona State Routes") and highways, such as the [Loop 101](/wiki/Arizona_State_Route_101 "Arizona State Route 101"), which is part of Phoenix's vast [freeway system](/wiki/Metropolitan_Phoenix_Freeways "Metropolitan Phoenix Freeways").[[122]](#cite_note-128)

### Public transportation, Amtrak, and intercity bus

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Arizona&action=edit&section=21 "Edit section: Public transportation, Amtrak, and intercity bus")]

See also: [List of passenger train stations in Arizona](/wiki/List_of_passenger_train_stations_in_Arizona "List of passenger train stations in Arizona")

The Phoenix and Tucson metropolitan areas are served by public bus transit systems. Yuma and Flagstaff also have public bus systems. [Greyhound Lines](/wiki/Greyhound_Lines "Greyhound Lines") serves Phoenix, Tucson, Flagstaff, Yuma, and several smaller communities statewide.

A [light rail](/wiki/Light_rail "Light rail") system, called [Valley Metro Rail](/wiki/Valley_Metro_Rail "Valley Metro Rail"), was completed in December 2008; it connects Central Phoenix with the nearby cities of Mesa and Tempe.[[123]](#cite_note-129)

In Tucson, the [Sun Link](/wiki/Sun_Link "Sun Link") streetcar system travels through the downtown area, connecting the main [University of Arizona](/wiki/University_of_Arizona "University of Arizona") campus with Mercado San Agustin on the western edge of downtown Tucson. Sun Link, loosely based on the [Portland Streetcar](/wiki/Portland_Streetcar "Portland Streetcar"), launched in July 2014.[[124]](#cite_note-130)

[Amtrak](/wiki/Amtrak "Amtrak") *[Southwest Chief](/wiki/Southwest_Chief "Southwest Chief")* route serves the northern part of the state, stopping at [Winslow](/wiki/Winslow_(Amtrak_station) "Winslow (Amtrak station)"), [Flagstaff](/wiki/Flagstaff_(Amtrak_station) "Flagstaff (Amtrak station)"), [Williams](/wiki/Williams_Junction_(Amtrak_station) "Williams Junction (Amtrak station)") and [Kingman](/wiki/Kingman_(Amtrak_station) "Kingman (Amtrak station)"). The *[Texas Eagle](/wiki/Texas_Eagle "Texas Eagle")* and *[Sunset Limited](/wiki/Sunset_Limited "Sunset Limited")* routes serve South-Central Arizona, stopping at [Tucson](/wiki/Tucson_(Amtrak_station) "Tucson (Amtrak station)"), [Maricopa](/wiki/Maricopa_(Amtrak_station) "Maricopa (Amtrak station)"), [Yuma](/wiki/Yuma_(Amtrak_station) "Yuma (Amtrak station)") and [Benson](/wiki/Benson_(Amtrak_station) "Benson (Amtrak station)"). Phoenix lost Amtrak service in 1996 with the rerouting of the *Sunset Limited*, and now an Amtrak bus runs between Phoenix and the station in Maricopa. As of 2021, Amtrak has proposed to restore rail service between Phoenix and Tucson.[[125]](#cite_note-131)

### Capitol complex

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Arizona&action=edit&section=23 "Edit section: Capitol complex")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/1/12/Azcap.jpg/250px-Azcap.jpg)](/wiki/File:Azcap.jpg)

The original [Arizona State Capitol](/wiki/Arizona_State_Capitol "Arizona State Capitol") in [Phoenix](/wiki/Phoenix,_Arizona "Phoenix, Arizona")

The capital of Arizona is [Phoenix](/wiki/Phoenix,_Arizona "Phoenix, Arizona"). The original [Capitol building](/wiki/Arizona_State_Capitol "Arizona State Capitol"), with its distinctive copper dome, was dedicated in 1901 (construction was completed for $136,000 in 1900) when the area was a territory. Phoenix became the official state capital with Arizona's admission to the union in 1912.[[126]](#cite_note-132)

The [House of Representatives](/wiki/Arizona_House_of_Representatives "Arizona House of Representatives") and [Senate](/wiki/Arizona_Senate "Arizona Senate") buildings were dedicated in 1960, and an Executive Office Building was dedicated in 1974 (the ninth floor of this building is where the Office of the Governor is located). The original Capitol building was converted into a museum.

The Capitol complex is fronted and highlighted by the richly landscaped [Wesley Bolin Memorial Plaza](/wiki/Wesley_Bolin_Memorial_Plaza "Wesley Bolin Memorial Plaza"), named after [Wesley Bolin](/wiki/Wesley_Bolin "Wesley Bolin"), a governor who died in office in the 1970s. The site also includes many monuments and memorials, including the anchor and signal mast from the [USS *Arizona*](/wiki/USS_Arizona_(BB-39) "USS Arizona (BB-39)") (one of the U.S. Navy ships [sunk in Pearl Harbor](/wiki/Attack_on_Pearl_Harbor "Attack on Pearl Harbor")) and a granite version of the [Ten Commandments](/wiki/Ten_Commandments "Ten Commandments").

### State legislative branch

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Arizona&action=edit&section=24 "Edit section: State legislative branch")]

The [Arizona Legislature](/wiki/Arizona_Legislature "Arizona Legislature") is [bicameral](/wiki/Bicameral "Bicameral") and consists of a thirty-member Senate and a 60-member House of Representatives. Each of the thirty legislative districts has one senator and two representatives. Legislators are elected for two-year terms.[[127]](#cite_note-133)

Each Legislature covers a two-year period. The first session following the general election is known as the first regular session, and the session convening in the second year is known as the second regular session. Each regular session begins on the second Monday in January and adjourns *sine die* (terminates for the year) no later than Saturday of the week in which the 100th day from the beginning of the regular session falls. The President of the Senate and Speaker of the House, by rule, may extend the session up to seven additional days. Thereafter, the session can be extended only by a majority vote of members present of each house.

The majority party is the [Republican Party](/wiki/United_States_Republican_Party "United States Republican Party"), which has held power in both houses since 1993. The [Democratic Party](/wiki/United_States_Democratic_Party "United States Democratic Party") picked up several legislative seats in recent elections, bringing both chambers one seat away from being equally divided as of 2021.[[128]](#cite_note-134)

Arizona state senators and representatives are elected for two-year terms and are limited to four consecutive terms in a chamber, though there is no limit on the total number of terms. When a lawmaker is term-limited from office, it is common for him or her to run for election in the other chamber.

Arizona's executive branch is headed by a [governor](/wiki/Governor_of_Arizona "Governor of Arizona"), who is elected to a four-year term. The governor may serve any number of terms, though no more than two in a row. Arizona is one of the few states that has no governor's mansion. During their term, the governors reside within their private residence, with executive offices housed in the executive tower at the state capitol. The governor of Arizona is [Katie Hobbs](/wiki/Katie_Hobbs "Katie Hobbs") (D).

Governor [Jan Brewer](/wiki/Jan_Brewer "Jan Brewer") assumed office in 2009 after [Janet Napolitano](/wiki/Janet_Napolitano "Janet Napolitano") had her nomination by Barack Obama for [Secretary of Homeland Security](/wiki/United_States_Secretary_of_Homeland_Security "United States Secretary of Homeland Security") confirmed by the Senate.[[129]](#cite_note-135) Arizona has had four female governors and a fifth currently serving, more than any other state.

Other elected executive officials include the [Secretary of State](/wiki/Secretary_of_State_(U.S._state_government) "Secretary of State (U.S. state government)"), [State Treasurer](/wiki/State_Treasurer "State Treasurer"), [State Attorney General](/wiki/Arizona_Attorney_General "Arizona Attorney General"), [Superintendent of Public Instruction](/wiki/State_education_agency "State education agency"), [State Mine Inspector](/wiki/Arizona_State_Mine_Inspector "Arizona State Mine Inspector"), and a five-member [Corporation Commission](/wiki/Arizona_Corporation_Commission "Arizona Corporation Commission"). All elected officials hold a term of four years, and are limited to two consecutive terms (except the office of the State Mine Inspector, which is limited to four terms).[[130]](#cite_note-136)

Arizona has the distinction of having the most female governors of any state with five having served, the first and only state to have three consecutive female governors,[[131]](#cite_note-137) and in 1998 elected women to all five top, statewide elected offices with the election of [Jane Dee Hull](/wiki/Jane_Dee_Hull "Jane Dee Hull") as Governor, [Betsey Bayless](/wiki/Betsey_Bayless "Betsey Bayless") as Secretary of State, [Janet Napolitano](/wiki/Janet_Napolitano "Janet Napolitano") as Attorney General, [Carol Springer](/wiki/Carol_Springer "Carol Springer") as Treasurer, and [Lisa Graham Keegan](/wiki/Lisa_Graham_Keegan "Lisa Graham Keegan") as Superintendent of Public Instruction.[[132]](#cite_note-138)

Arizona is one of five states that do not have a [lieutenant governor](/wiki/Lieutenant_governor_(United_States)#Arizona "Lieutenant governor (United States)"). The elected secretary of state is first in line to succeed the governor in the event of death, disability, resignation, or removal from office. If appointed, the Secretary of State is not eligible and the next governor is selected from the next eligible official in the line of succession, including the attorney general, state treasurer, and superintendent of public instruction. Since 1977, four secretaries of state and one attorney general have succeeded to the state's governorship.

On November 8, 2022, Arizona voters approved a state constitutional amendment (Proposition 131) that created the position and office of the lieutenant governor beginning with the 2026 elections. The position will be elected on a joint ticket with the governor. The lieutenant governor ascends to the governorship if the incumbent governor dies, resigns, or is removed (via impeachment conviction) from office. The proposition, through a law pre-passed by the state legislature, also tasks the governor with assigning a job to her or his running mate, such as chief of staff, the director of the state Department of Administration, or "any position" to which the governor can appoint someone by law.[[133]](#cite_note-139)

### State judicial branch

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Arizona&action=edit&section=26 "Edit section: State judicial branch")]

The [Arizona Supreme Court](/wiki/Arizona_Supreme_Court "Arizona Supreme Court") is the highest court in Arizona, consisting of a chief justice, a vice chief justice, and five associate justices. Justices are appointed by the governor from a list recommended by a bipartisan commission and must be sustained in office by election after the first two years following their appointment. Subsequent sustaining elections occur every six years. The supreme court has appellate jurisdiction in death penalty cases, but nearly all other appellate cases go through the [Arizona Court of Appeals](/wiki/Arizona_Court_of_Appeals "Arizona Court of Appeals") first. The court has original jurisdiction in a few other circumstances, as outlined in the state constitution. The court meets in the Arizona Supreme Court Building at the capitol complex (at the southern end of Wesley Bolin Plaza).

The Arizona Court of Appeals, subdivided into two divisions, is the intermediate court in the state. Division One is based in Phoenix, consists of nineteen judges, and has jurisdiction in the Western and Northern regions of the state, along with the greater Phoenix area. Division Two is based in Tucson, consists of nine judges, and has jurisdiction over the Southern regions of the state, including the Tucson area. Judges are selected in a method similar to the one used for state supreme court justices.

Each county of Arizona has a [superior court](/wiki/Arizona_Superior_Court "Arizona Superior Court"), the size and organization of which are varied and generally depend on the size of the particular county.

### Federal representation

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Arizona&action=edit&section=28 "Edit section: Federal representation")]

Arizona's two United States senators are and [Mark Kelly](/wiki/Mark_Kelly "Mark Kelly") (D) and [Ruben Gallego](/wiki/Ruben_Gallego "Ruben Gallego") (D).

Arizona's United States representatives are [David Schweikert](/wiki/David_Schweikert "David Schweikert") (R-1), [Eli Crane](/wiki/Eli_Crane "Eli Crane") (R-2), [Yassamin Ansari](/wiki/Yassamin_Ansari "Yassamin Ansari") (D-3), [Greg Stanton](/wiki/Greg_Stanton "Greg Stanton") (D-4), [Andy Biggs](/wiki/Andy_Biggs "Andy Biggs") (R-5), [Juan Ciscomani](/wiki/Juan_Ciscomani "Juan Ciscomani") (R-6), [Adelita Grijalva](/wiki/Adelita_Grijalva "Adelita Grijalva") (D-7), [Abraham Hamadeh](/wiki/Abraham_Hamadeh "Abraham Hamadeh") (R-8), and [Paul Gosar](/wiki/Paul_Gosar "Paul Gosar") (R-9). Arizona gained a ninth seat in the House of Representatives due to [redistricting](/wiki/Redistricting "Redistricting") based on the [2010 United States census](/wiki/2010_United_States_census "2010 United States census").
