From statehood through the late 1940s, Arizona was primarily dominated by the [Democratic Party](/wiki/United_States_Democratic_Party "United States Democratic Party"). During this time, the Democratic candidate for the presidency carried the state each election, the only exceptions being the elections of [1920](/wiki/1920_United_States_presidential_election "1920 United States presidential election"), [1924](/wiki/1924_United_States_presidential_election "1924 United States presidential election") and [1928](/wiki/1928_United_States_presidential_election "1928 United States presidential election") – all three were national [Republican](/wiki/Republican_Party_(United_States) "Republican Party (United States)") landslides.

In 1924, Congress had passed a law granting citizenship and suffrage to all Native Americans, some of whom had previously been excluded as members of tribes on reservations. Legal interpretations of Arizona's constitution prohibited Native Americans living on reservations from voting, classifying them as being under "guardianship".[[50]](#cite_note-HAvote-53) This interpretation was overturned as being incorrect and unconstitutional in 1948 by the Arizona Supreme Court, following a lawsuit by World War II Indian veterans [Frank Harrison](/w/index.php?title=Frank_Harrison_(soldier)&action=edit&redlink=1 "Frank Harrison (soldier) (page does not exist)") and Harry Austin, both of the [Fort McDowell Yavapai Nation](/wiki/Fort_McDowell_Yavapai_Nation "Fort McDowell Yavapai Nation"). The landmark case is *[Harrison and Austin v. Laveen](/wiki/Harrison_and_Austin_v._Laveen "Harrison and Austin v. Laveen")*. After the men were refused the opportunity to register in Maricopa County, they filed lawsuit against the registrar. The [National Congress of American Indians](/wiki/National_Congress_of_American_Indians "National Congress of American Indians"), the [Department of Justice](/wiki/United_States_Department_of_Justice "United States Department of Justice"), the [Department of the Interior](/wiki/Department_of_the_Interior "Department of the Interior"), and the [American Civil Liberties Union](/wiki/American_Civil_Liberties_Union "American Civil Liberties Union") all filed *amicus curiae* (friends of the court) briefs in the case. The State Supreme Court established the rights of Native Americans to vote in the state; at the time, they comprised about 11% of the population.[[50]](#cite_note-HAvote-53) That year, a similar provision was overturned in New Mexico when challenged by another Indian veteran in court. These were the only two states that had continued to prohibit Native Americans from voting.[[136]](#cite_note-NAvote-142)[[50]](#cite_note-HAvote-53)

Arizona voted Republican in every presidential election from 1952 to 1992, with [Richard Nixon](/wiki/Richard_Nixon "Richard Nixon") and [Ronald Reagan](/wiki/Ronald_Reagan "Ronald Reagan") winning the state by particularly large margins. During this forty-year span, it was the only state not to be carried by a Democrat at least once.

Democrat [Lyndon Johnson](/wiki/Lyndon_Johnson "Lyndon Johnson"), in [1964](/wiki/1964_United_States_presidential_election "1964 United States presidential election"), lost the state by fewer than 5,000 votes to Arizona Senator and native [Barry Goldwater](/wiki/Barry_Goldwater "Barry Goldwater"). (This was the most closely contested state in what was otherwise a landslide victory for Johnson that year.) Democrat [Bill Clinton](/wiki/Bill_Clinton "Bill Clinton") ended this streak in [1996](/wiki/1996_United_States_presidential_election "1996 United States presidential election"), when he won Arizona by a little over two percentage points (Clinton had previously come within less than two percent of winning Arizona's electoral votes in [1992](/wiki/1992_United_States_presidential_election "1992 United States presidential election")). From 2000 until 2016, the majority of the state continued to support Republican presidential candidates by solid margins. In the [2020 United States presidential election](/wiki/2020_United_States_presidential_election "2020 United States presidential election"), [Joe Biden](/wiki/Joe_Biden "Joe Biden") again broke the streak by becoming the first Democratic presidential candidate to win Arizona since 1996.[[137]](#cite_note-143)

Since the mid 20th century, the Republican Party has also dominated Arizona politics in general. The fast-growing Phoenix and Tucson suburbs became reliably Republican areas from the 1950s onward. During this time, many "Pinto Democrats", or conservative Democrats from rural areas, became increasingly willing to support Republicans at the state and national level. While the state normally supports Republicans at the federal level, Democrats are often competitive in statewide elections. Two of the last six governors have been Democrats.

On March 4, 2008, Senator [John McCain](/wiki/John_McCain "John McCain") effectively clinched the Republican nomination for 2008, becoming the first major party presidential nominee from the state since Barry Goldwater in 1964. McCain won Arizona by eight percentage points in the 2008 General Election.

Arizona politics is dominated by a longstanding rivalry between its two largest counties, [Maricopa](/wiki/Maricopa_County "Maricopa County") and [Pima](/wiki/Pima_County "Pima County") – home to Phoenix and Tucson, respectively. The two counties have almost 75 percent of the state's population and cast almost 80 percent of the state's vote. They also elect a substantial majority of the state legislature.

Maricopa County is home to almost 60 percent of the state's population, and most of the state's elected officials live there. Before [Joe Biden](/wiki/Joe_Biden "Joe Biden") won Maricopa County in 2020, it had voted Republican in every presidential election since 1952. This includes the [1964](/wiki/1964_United_States_presidential_election "1964 United States presidential election") run of native son Barry Goldwater; he would not have carried his home state without his 20,000-vote margin in Maricopa County.

In the 2024 General Election, former president Donald Trump won Maricopa County by 71,515 votes and won the state of Arizona by 187,382 votes, making the 5.5 percent statewide victory the largest percentage win of any of the seven 2024 swing states.[[138]](#cite_note-144)

Pima County, home to Tucson, and most of southern Arizona have historically voted more Democratic. While Tucson's suburbs lean Republican, they hold to a somewhat more moderate brand of Republicanism than is common in the Phoenix area.

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/9/96/-RedForEd_%2841008219574%29.jpg/250px--RedForEd_%2841008219574%29.jpg)](/wiki/File:-RedForEd_(41008219574).jpg)

[Arizona teacher's strike](/wiki/2018_Arizona_teachers%27_strike "2018 Arizona teachers' strike") and rally on April 26, 2018

In 2010, Arizona adopted [SB 1070](/wiki/Arizona_SB_1070 "Arizona SB 1070"), called the "toughest [immigration](/wiki/Immigration_to_the_United_States "Immigration to the United States") law" in the United States. A fierce debate erupted between supporters and detractors of SB 1070.[[139]](#cite_note-145) The [United States Supreme Court](/wiki/United_States_Supreme_Court "United States Supreme Court") struck down portions of the Arizona law, which required all immigrants to carry immigration papers at all times, in *[Arizona v. United States](/wiki/Arizona_v._United_States "Arizona v. United States")*.[[140]](#cite_note-146)

The [West Virginia](/wiki/2018_West_Virginia_teachers%27_strike "2018 West Virginia teachers' strike") teachers' strike in 2018 inspired [teachers in other states](/wiki/2018%E2%80%9319_education_workers%27_strikes_in_the_United_States "2018–19 education workers' strikes in the United States"), including [Arizona](/wiki/2018_Arizona_teachers%27_strike "2018 Arizona teachers' strike"), to take similar action.[[141]](#cite_note-147)

Arizona retains the death penalty. Authorized methods of execution include the [gas chamber](/wiki/Gas_chamber "Gas chamber").[[142]](#cite_note-148)

In 2024, Arizona voters passed a [constitutional amendment](/wiki/2024_Arizona_Proposition_139 "2024 Arizona Proposition 139") recognising abortion access within the Arizona state constitution,[[143]](#cite_note-149) becoming the southernmost continental state to codify abortion rights.[[144]](#cite_note-150)

### Same-sex marriage and civil unions

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Arizona&action=edit&section=30 "Edit section: Same-sex marriage and civil unions")]

Arizona was initially the first U.S. state to reject a same-sex marriage ban in 2006 (proposition failed, 48%–52%) but passed a constitutional amendment prohibiting same-sex marriage two years later (56%–44%) with proponents enjoying a 10-to-1 advertising advantage and benefitting from a broadcast message from [Roman Catholic Church](/wiki/Roman_Catholic_Church "Roman Catholic Church") Bishop [Thomas Olmsted](/wiki/Thomas_Olmsted "Thomas Olmsted") of Phoenix, calling the issue "non-negotiable" for Catholics and played at every mass in its jurisdiction.[[145]](#cite_note-151) Several cities created civil unions in the years following the marriage ban.[[146]](#cite_note-152) Following a federal district court ruling in October 2014, Arizona's Attorney General stopped objecting to same-sex marriage, making Arizona the 31st state to legalize it.[[147]](#cite_note-153) Public opinion has shifted significantly, with 2023 polling showing overwhelming support for same-sex marriage among Arizonans.[[148]](#cite_note-154)

## Education

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Arizona&action=edit&section=31 "Edit section: Education")]

### Elementary and secondary education

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Arizona&action=edit&section=32 "Edit section: Elementary and secondary education")]

See also: [List of school districts in Arizona](/wiki/List_of_school_districts_in_Arizona "List of school districts in Arizona") and [List of high schools in Arizona](/wiki/List_of_high_schools_in_Arizona "List of high schools in Arizona")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/5/54/Arizona_School_Districts.png/250px-Arizona_School_Districts.png)](/wiki/File:Arizona_School_Districts.png)

[Arizona School Districts](/wiki/List_of_school_districts_in_Arizona "List of school districts in Arizona")

Public schools in Arizona are separated into about 220 local school districts which operate independently, but are governed in most cases by elected county school superintendents; these are in turn overseen by the Arizona State Board of Education and the [Arizona Department of Education](/wiki/Arizona_Department_of_Education "Arizona Department of Education"). A state [Superintendent of Public Instruction](/wiki/Arizona_Superintendent_of_Public_Instruction "Arizona Superintendent of Public Instruction") (elected in partisan elections every even-numbered year when there is not a presidential election, for a four-year term). In 2005, a School District Redistricting Commission was established with the goal of combining and consolidating many of these districts.[[149]](#cite_note-155)

### Higher education

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Arizona&action=edit&section=33 "Edit section: Higher education")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/a/a6/University_of_Arizona_mall.jpg/250px-University_of_Arizona_mall.jpg)](/wiki/File:University_of_Arizona_mall.jpg)

The [University of Arizona](/wiki/University_of_Arizona "University of Arizona") (the Mall) in [Tucson](/wiki/Tucson,_Arizona "Tucson, Arizona")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/c/c3/Asubiodesign.jpg/250px-Asubiodesign.jpg)](/wiki/File:Asubiodesign.jpg)

[Arizona State University](/wiki/Arizona_State_University "Arizona State University") (a biodesign building) in [Tempe](/wiki/Tempe,_Arizona "Tempe, Arizona")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/b/bd/Walkup_Skydome.jpg/250px-Walkup_Skydome.jpg)](/wiki/File:Walkup_Skydome.jpg)

[Northern Arizona University](/wiki/Northern_Arizona_University "Northern Arizona University") (The Skydome) in [Flagstaff](/wiki/Flagstaff,_Arizona "Flagstaff, Arizona")

Arizona is served by three public universities: The [University of Arizona](/wiki/University_of_Arizona "University of Arizona"), [Arizona State University](/wiki/Arizona_State_University "Arizona State University"), and [Northern Arizona University](/wiki/Northern_Arizona_University "Northern Arizona University"). These schools are governed by the [Arizona Board of Regents](/wiki/Arizona_Board_of_Regents "Arizona Board of Regents").

Private higher education in Arizona is dominated by a large number of for-profit and "chain" (multi-site) universities.[[150]](#cite_note-156)

[Embry–Riddle Aeronautical University, Prescott](/wiki/Embry%E2%80%93Riddle_Aeronautical_University,_Prescott "Embry–Riddle Aeronautical University, Prescott") and [Prescott College](/wiki/Prescott_College "Prescott College") are Arizona's only non-profit four-year private colleges.[[151]](#cite_note-157)

Arizona has a wide network of two-year vocational schools and [community colleges](/wiki/Community_colleges "Community colleges"). These colleges were governed historically by a separate statewide board of directors but, in 2002, the state legislature transferred almost all oversight authority to individual community college districts.[[152]](#cite_note-158) The Maricopa County Community College District includes 11 community colleges throughout Maricopa County and is one of the largest in the nation.

### Visual arts and museums

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Arizona&action=edit&section=38 "Edit section: Visual arts and museums")]

See also: [List of museums in Arizona](/wiki/List_of_museums_in_Arizona "List of museums in Arizona")

[Phoenix Art Museum](/wiki/Phoenix_Art_Museum "Phoenix Art Museum"), on the historic [Central Avenue Corridor](/wiki/Central_Avenue_Corridor "Central Avenue Corridor") in Phoenix, is the Southwest's largest collection of visual art from across the world. The museum displays international exhibitions alongside the museum's collection of more than 18,000 works of American, Asian, European, Latin American, Western American, modern and contemporary art, and fashion design. With a community education mandate since 1951, Phoenix Art Museum holds a year-round program of festivals, live performances, independent art films and educational programs. The museum also has PhxArtKids, an interactive space for children; photography exhibitions through the museum's partnership with the [Center for Creative Photography](/wiki/Center_for_Creative_Photography "Center for Creative Photography"); the landscaped Sculpture Garden and dining at Arcadia Farms.[[154]](#cite_note-160)

Arizona is a recognized center of Native American art, with a number of galleries showcasing historical and contemporary works. The [Heard Museum](/wiki/Heard_Museum "Heard Museum"), also in Phoenix, is a major repository of Native American art. Some of the signature exhibits include a full Navajo hogan, the Mareen Allen Nichols Collection containing 260 pieces of contemporary jewelry, the [Barry Goldwater](/wiki/Barry_Goldwater "Barry Goldwater") Collection of 437 historic [Hopi kachina dolls](/wiki/Hopi_kachina_dolls "Hopi kachina dolls"), and an exhibit on the 19th-century boarding school experiences of Native Americans. The Heard Museum has about 250,000 visitors a year.

[Sedona](/wiki/Sedona,_Arizona "Sedona, Arizona"), [Jerome](/wiki/Jerome,_Arizona "Jerome, Arizona"), and [Tubac](/wiki/Tubac,_Arizona "Tubac, Arizona") are known as budding artist colonies, and small arts scenes exist in the larger cities and near the state universities.[[155]](#cite_note-161)

### Film

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Arizona&action=edit&section=39 "Edit section: Film")]

See also: [List of films shot in Arizona](/wiki/List_of_films_shot_in_Arizona "List of films shot in Arizona")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/3/33/Monument_Valley_15.jpg/250px-Monument_Valley_15.jpg)](/wiki/File:Monument_Valley_15.jpg)

View of Monument Valley from [John Ford](/wiki/John_Ford "John Ford")'s Point

Several major Hollywood films, such as *[Billy Jack](/wiki/Billy_Jack "Billy Jack")*, *[U Turn](/wiki/U_Turn_(1997_film) "U Turn (1997 film)")*, *[Waiting to Exhale](/wiki/Waiting_to_Exhale "Waiting to Exhale")*, *[Just One of the Guys](/wiki/Just_One_of_the_Guys "Just One of the Guys")*, *[Can't Buy Me Love](/wiki/Can%27t_Buy_Me_Love_(film) "Can't Buy Me Love (film)")*, *[Bill & Ted's Excellent Adventure](/wiki/Bill_%26_Ted%27s_Excellent_Adventure "Bill & Ted's Excellent Adventure")*, *[The Scorpion King](/wiki/The_Scorpion_King "The Scorpion King")*, *[The Banger Sisters](/wiki/The_Banger_Sisters "The Banger Sisters")*, *[Used Cars](/wiki/Used_Cars "Used Cars")*, and *[Raising Arizona](/wiki/Raising_Arizona "Raising Arizona")* have been made there (as have many [Westerns](/wiki/Western_(genre) "Western (genre)")). The 1993 science fiction movie *[Fire in the Sky](/wiki/Fire_in_the_Sky "Fire in the Sky")*, based on a reported alien abduction in the town of [Snowflake](/wiki/Snowflake,_Arizona "Snowflake, Arizona"), was set in Snowflake. It was filmed in the Oregon towns of [Oakland](/wiki/Oakland,_Oregon "Oakland, Oregon"), [Roseburg](/wiki/Roseburg,_Oregon "Roseburg, Oregon"), and [Sutherlin](/wiki/Sutherlin,_Oregon "Sutherlin, Oregon").

The 1974 film *[Alice Doesn't Live Here Anymore](/wiki/Alice_Doesn%27t_Live_Here_Anymore "Alice Doesn't Live Here Anymore")*, for which [Ellen Burstyn](/wiki/Ellen_Burstyn "Ellen Burstyn") won the [Academy Award for Best Actress](/wiki/Academy_Award_for_Best_Actress "Academy Award for Best Actress"), and also starring [Kris Kristofferson](/wiki/Kris_Kristofferson "Kris Kristofferson"), was set in Tucson. The climax of the 1977 [Clint Eastwood](/wiki/Clint_Eastwood "Clint Eastwood") film *[The Gauntlet](/wiki/The_Gauntlet_(film) "The Gauntlet (film)")* takes place in downtown Phoenix. The final segments of the 1984 film *[Starman](/wiki/Starman_(film) "Starman (film)")* take place at [Meteor Crater](/wiki/Meteor_Crater "Meteor Crater") outside [Winslow](/wiki/Winslow,_Arizona "Winslow, Arizona"). The [Jeff Foxworthy](/wiki/Jeff_Foxworthy "Jeff Foxworthy") comedy documentary movie *[Blue Collar Comedy Tour](/wiki/Blue_Collar_Comedy_Tour:_The_Movie "Blue Collar Comedy Tour: The Movie")* was filmed almost entirely at the [Dodge Theatre](/wiki/Dodge_Theatre "Dodge Theatre"). Some of [Alfred Hitchcock](/wiki/Alfred_Hitchcock "Alfred Hitchcock")'s classic film *[Psycho](/wiki/Psycho_(1960_film) "Psycho (1960 film)")* was shot in Phoenix, the ostensible home town of the main character.

Some of the television shows filmed or set in Arizona include *[The New Dick Van Dyke Show](/wiki/The_New_Dick_Van_Dyke_Show "The New Dick Van Dyke Show")*, *[Medium](/wiki/Medium_(TV_Series) "Medium (TV Series)")*, *[Alice](/wiki/Alice_(American_TV_series) "Alice (American TV series)")*, *[The First 48](/wiki/The_First_48 "The First 48")*, *[Insomniac with Dave Attell](/wiki/Insomniac_with_Dave_Attell "Insomniac with Dave Attell")*, *[Cops](/wiki/Cops_(TV_series) "Cops (TV series)")*, and *[America's Most Wanted](/wiki/America%27s_Most_Wanted "America's Most Wanted")*. The TV sitcom *Alice*, which was based on the movie was set in Phoenix. *[Twilight](/wiki/Twilight_(2008_film) "Twilight (2008 film)")* had passages set in Phoenix at the beginning and the end of the film.

### Music

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Arizona&action=edit&section=40 "Edit section: Music")]

Main article: [Music of Arizona](/wiki/Music_of_Arizona "Music of Arizona")

Arizona is prominently featured in the lyrics of many [Country and Western](/wiki/Country_and_Western "Country and Western") songs, such as [Jamie O'Neal](/wiki/Jamie_O%27Neal "Jamie O'Neal")'s hit ballad "[There Is No Arizona](/wiki/There_Is_No_Arizona "There Is No Arizona")". [George Strait](/wiki/George_Strait "George Strait")'s "Oceanfront Property" uses "ocean front property in Arizona" as a metaphor for a sucker proposition. The line "see you down in Arizona Bay" is used in a [Tool](/wiki/Tool_(band) "Tool (band)") song in reference to the possibility (expressed as a *hope* by comedian [Bill Hicks](/wiki/Bill_Hicks "Bill Hicks")) that Southern California will one day fall into the ocean. [Glen Campbell](/wiki/Glen_Campbell "Glen Campbell"), a notable resident, popularized the song "[By The Time I Get To Phoenix](/wiki/By_The_Time_I_Get_To_Phoenix "By The Time I Get To Phoenix")".

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/e/ed/TakeItEasy_WinslowAZ.jpg/250px-TakeItEasy_WinslowAZ.jpg)](/wiki/File:TakeItEasy_WinslowAZ.jpg)

[Standin' on the Corner Park](/wiki/Standin%27_on_the_Corner_Park "Standin' on the Corner Park") and mural in [Winslow, Arizona](/wiki/Winslow,_Arizona "Winslow, Arizona")

"Arizona" was the title of a popular song recorded by [Mark Lindsay](/wiki/Mark_Lindsay "Mark Lindsay"). Arizona is mentioned by the hit song "[Take It Easy](/wiki/Take_It_Easy "Take It Easy")", written by [Jackson Browne](/wiki/Jackson_Browne "Jackson Browne") and [Glenn Frey](/wiki/Glenn_Frey "Glenn Frey") and performed by the [Eagles](/wiki/Eagles_(band) "Eagles (band)"). Arizona is also mentioned in the Beatles' song "Get Back", credited to John Lennon and Paul McCartney; McCartney sings: "JoJo left his home in Tucson, Arizona, for some California grass." "[Carefree Highway](/wiki/Carefree_Highway_(song) "Carefree Highway (song)")", released in 1974 by [Gordon Lightfoot](/wiki/Gordon_Lightfoot "Gordon Lightfoot"), takes its name from [Arizona State Route 74](/wiki/Arizona_State_Route_74 "Arizona State Route 74") north of Phoenix.[[156]](#cite_note-Crawdaddy-162)

Arizona's budding music scene is helped by emerging bands, as well as some well-known artists. The [Gin Blossoms](/wiki/Gin_Blossoms "Gin Blossoms"), [Chronic Future](/wiki/Chronic_Future "Chronic Future"), [Roger Clyne and the Peacemakers](/wiki/Roger_Clyne_and_the_Peacemakers "Roger Clyne and the Peacemakers"), [Jimmy Eat World](/wiki/Jimmy_Eat_World "Jimmy Eat World"), [Caroline's Spine](/wiki/Caroline%27s_Spine "Caroline's Spine"), and others began their careers in Arizona. Also, a number of [punk](/wiki/Punk_rock "Punk rock") and rock bands got their start in Arizona, including [JFA](/wiki/JFA_(band) "JFA (band)"), [The Feederz](/wiki/The_Feederz "The Feederz"), [Sun City Girls](/wiki/Sun_City_Girls "Sun City Girls"), [The Meat Puppets](/wiki/The_Meat_Puppets "The Meat Puppets"), [The Maine](/wiki/The_Maine_(band) "The Maine (band)"), [The Summer Set](/wiki/The_Summer_Set "The Summer Set"), and more recently [Authority Zero](/wiki/Authority_Zero "Authority Zero") and [Digital Summer](/wiki/Digital_Summer "Digital Summer").

Arizona also has many singers and other musicians. Singer, songwriter and guitarist [Michelle Branch](/wiki/Michelle_Branch "Michelle Branch") is from [Sedona](/wiki/Sedona,_Arizona "Sedona, Arizona"). [Chester Bennington](/wiki/Chester_Bennington "Chester Bennington"), the former lead vocalist of [Linkin Park](/wiki/Linkin_Park "Linkin Park"), and [mash-up](/wiki/Mashup_(music) "Mashup (music)") artist [DJ Z-Trip](/wiki/DJ_Z-Trip "DJ Z-Trip") are both from [Phoenix](/wiki/Phoenix,_Arizona "Phoenix, Arizona"). One of Arizona's better known musicians is [shock rocker](/wiki/Shock_rock "Shock rock") [Alice Cooper](/wiki/Alice_Cooper "Alice Cooper"), who helped define the genre. [Maynard James Keenan](/wiki/Maynard_James_Keenan "Maynard James Keenan"), the lead singer of the bands [Tool](/wiki/Tool_(band) "Tool (band)"), [A Perfect Circle](/wiki/A_Perfect_Circle "A Perfect Circle"), and [Puscifer](/wiki/Puscifer "Puscifer"), calls the town of [Cornville](/wiki/Cornville,_Arizona "Cornville, Arizona") home.

Other notable singers include [country](/wiki/Country_music "Country music") singers [Dierks Bentley](/wiki/Dierks_Bentley "Dierks Bentley") and [Marty Robbins](/wiki/Marty_Robbins "Marty Robbins"), [folk](/wiki/Folk_music "Folk music") singer [Katie Lee](/wiki/Katie_Lee_(singer) "Katie Lee (singer)"), [Fleetwood Mac](/wiki/Fleetwood_Mac "Fleetwood Mac")'s [Stevie Nicks](/wiki/Stevie_Nicks "Stevie Nicks"), [CeCe Peniston](/wiki/CeCe_Peniston "CeCe Peniston"), [Rex Allen](/wiki/Rex_Allen "Rex Allen"), 2007 *[American Idol](/wiki/American_Idol_(season_6) "American Idol (season 6)")* winner [Jordin Sparks](/wiki/Jordin_Sparks "Jordin Sparks"), and [Linda Ronstadt](/wiki/Linda_Ronstadt "Linda Ronstadt").

Arizona is also known for its [heavy metal](/wiki/Heavy_metal_music "Heavy metal music") scene, which is centered in and around Phoenix. In the early to mid-1990s, it included bands such as [Job for a Cowboy](/wiki/Job_for_a_Cowboy "Job for a Cowboy"), [Knights of the Abyss](/wiki/Knights_of_the_Abyss "Knights of the Abyss"), [Greeley Estates](/wiki/Greeley_Estates "Greeley Estates"), [Eyes Set To Kill](/wiki/Eyes_Set_To_Kill "Eyes Set To Kill"), [blessthefall](/wiki/Blessthefall "Blessthefall"), [The Word Alive](/wiki/The_Word_Alive "The Word Alive"), [The Dead Rabbitts](/wiki/The_Dead_Rabbitts "The Dead Rabbitts"), and [Abigail Williams](/wiki/Abigail_Williams_(band) "Abigail Williams (band)"). The bands [Soulfly](/wiki/Soulfly "Soulfly") and [Gatecreeper](/wiki/Gatecreeper "Gatecreeper") calls Phoenix home, and [Megadeth](/wiki/Megadeth "Megadeth") lived in Phoenix for about a decade. Around 2009, Phoenix began to host a burgeoning desert rock and sludge metal underground, (ala' Kyuss in 1990s California) led by bands like Wolves of Winter, Asimov, and Dead Canyon.

American composer [Elliott Carter](/wiki/Elliott_Carter "Elliott Carter") composed his first String Quartet (1950–51) while on sabbatical (from New York) in Arizona. The quartet won a [Pulitzer Prize](/wiki/Pulitzer_Prize "Pulitzer Prize") and other awards and is now a staple of the string quartet repertoire.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

Four [Super Bowls](/wiki/Super_Bowl "Super Bowl") have been held in Arizona, including [Super Bowl LVII](/wiki/Super_Bowl_LVII "Super Bowl LVII") which was held at [State Farm Stadium](/wiki/State_Farm_Stadium "State Farm Stadium") on February 12, 2023.[[157]](#cite_note-sb57sitechosen-163)

Due to its numerous golf courses, Arizona is home to several stops on the [PGA Tour](/wiki/PGA_Tour "PGA Tour"), most notably the [Phoenix Open](/wiki/Phoenix_Open "Phoenix Open"), held at the [TPC of Scottsdale](/wiki/TPC_of_Scottsdale "TPC of Scottsdale"), and the [WGC-Accenture Match Play Championship](/wiki/WGC-Accenture_Match_Play_Championship "WGC-Accenture Match Play Championship") at the [Ritz-Carlton Golf Club](/wiki/Ritz-Carlton_Golf_Club "Ritz-Carlton Golf Club") in [Marana](/wiki/Marana,_Arizona "Marana, Arizona").[[158]](#cite_note-164)

Auto racing is another sport known in the state. [Phoenix Raceway](/wiki/Phoenix_Raceway "Phoenix Raceway") in [Avondale](/wiki/Avondale,_Arizona "Avondale, Arizona") is home to [NASCAR](/wiki/NASCAR "NASCAR") race weekends twice a year. [Firebird International Raceway](/wiki/Firebird_International_Raceway "Firebird International Raceway") near [Chandler](/wiki/Chandler,_Arizona "Chandler, Arizona") is home to drag racing and other motorsport events.[[159]](#cite_note-165)

### College sports

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Arizona&action=edit&section=42 "Edit section: College sports")]

College sports are also prevalent in Arizona. The [Arizona State Sun Devils](/wiki/Arizona_State_Sun_Devils "Arizona State Sun Devils") and the [Arizona Wildcats](/wiki/Arizona_Wildcats "Arizona Wildcats") belong to the [Big-12 Conference](/wiki/Big-12_Conference "Big-12 Conference") while the [Northern Arizona Lumberjacks](/wiki/Northern_Arizona_Lumberjacks "Northern Arizona Lumberjacks") compete in the [Big Sky Conference](/wiki/Big_Sky_Conference "Big Sky Conference") and the [Grand Canyon Antelopes](/wiki/Grand_Canyon_Antelopes "Grand Canyon Antelopes") compete in the [Western Athletic Conference](/wiki/Western_Athletic_Conference "Western Athletic Conference"). The rivalry between Arizona State Sun Devils and the Arizona Wildcats predates Arizona's statehood, and is the oldest rivalry in the [NCAA](/wiki/National_Collegiate_Athletic_Association "National Collegiate Athletic Association").[[160]](#cite_note-166) The [Territorial Cup](/wiki/Territorial_Cup "Territorial Cup"), first awarded in 1889 and certified as the oldest trophy in college football,[[161]](#cite_note-167) is awarded to the winner of the annual football game between the two schools.

Arizona also hosts several college football [bowl games](/wiki/Bowl_game "Bowl game"). The [Fiesta Bowl](/wiki/Fiesta_Bowl "Fiesta Bowl"), originally held at [Sun Devil Stadium](/wiki/Sun_Devil_Stadium "Sun Devil Stadium"), is now held at [State Farm Stadium](/wiki/State_Farm_Stadium "State Farm Stadium") in [Glendale](/wiki/Glendale,_Arizona "Glendale, Arizona"). The Fiesta Bowl is part of the new [College Football Playoff](/wiki/College_Football_Playoff "College Football Playoff") (CFP). University of Phoenix Stadium was also home to the [2007](/wiki/2007_BCS_National_Championship_Game "2007 BCS National Championship Game") and [2011](/wiki/2011_BCS_National_Championship_Game "2011 BCS National Championship Game") [BCS National Championship Games](/wiki/BCS_National_Championship_Game "BCS National Championship Game").

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/2/2a/SpringTrainingHoHoKamPark.jpg/250px-SpringTrainingHoHoKamPark.jpg)](/wiki/File:SpringTrainingHoHoKamPark.jpg)

A spring training game between the Cubs and White Sox at [HoHoKam Park](/wiki/HoHoKam_Park "HoHoKam Park")

State Farm Stadium hosted the Final Four of the [NCAA Division I men's basketball tournament](/wiki/NCAA_Division_I_men%27s_basketball_tournament "NCAA Division I men's basketball tournament") in 2017 and is scheduled to host it again in 2024.[[162]](#cite_note-168)

### Baseball

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Arizona&action=edit&section=43 "Edit section: Baseball")]

Arizona is a popular location for [Major League Baseball](/wiki/Major_League_Baseball "Major League Baseball") [spring training](/wiki/Spring_training "Spring training"), as it is the site of the [Cactus League](/wiki/Cactus_League "Cactus League"). Spring training was first started in Arizona in 1947, when Brewers owner Veeck sold them in 1945 but went on to purchase the Cleveland Indians in 1946. He decided to train the Cleveland Indians in [Tucson](/wiki/Tucson "Tucson") and convinced the New York Giants to give [Phoenix](/wiki/Phoenix,_Arizona "Phoenix, Arizona") a try. Thus, the Cactus League was born.[[163]](#cite_note-169)

On March 9, 1995, Arizona was awarded a franchise to begin to play for the 1998 season. A$130 million franchise fee was paid to Major League Baseball and on January 16, 1997, the Diamondbacks were officially voted into the National League.

Since their debut, the [Diamondbacks](/wiki/Arizona_Diamondbacks "Arizona Diamondbacks") have won five National League West titles, two National League Championship pennants, and the [2001 World Series](/wiki/2001_World_Series "2001 World Series").[[164]](#cite_note-170)[[165]](#cite_note-171)
